summary: Production VAD/segmentation systems converge on a single shape that matches AutoSlicer v2's task: a tiny (0.1M-2M param) frame-level classifier over short windows, run as a sliding window over the whole file, with overlap-add averaging of per-frame probabilities, followed by hysteresis thresholding (separate onset/offset thresholds) plus min_duration_on/min_duration_off duration filtering and padding to turn frame probabilities into segments. Key verified facts: Silero VAD v5 is ~2MB (MIT), processes a 32ms chunk in <1ms on one CPU thread (RTS ~96-165), and exposes the full smoothing recipe (threshold=0.5, neg_threshold=threshold-0.15 hysteresis, min_speech_duration_ms=250, min_silence_duration_ms=100, speech_pad_ms=30, max_speech_duration_s splitting at last >100ms silence). pyannote segmentation-3.0 (PyanNet: SincNet 42.6K + 4-layer BiLSTM 1.4M + linear, ~1.5M total) ingests 10s windows and outputs 7-class POWERSET frame labels (non-speech / 3 speakers / 3 pairs) — the powerset multi-class trick is directly applicable to modeling our confusers as explicit classes; its pipeline stitches long audio with overlapping windows (diarization uses 90% overlap, skip_aggregation; VAD uses overlap-add averaging) and applies onset/offset/min_duration_on/min_duration_off hysteresis. NVIDIA Frame-VAD MarbleNet v2.0 proves 91.5K params suffice for 20ms-resolution frame classification on hour-scale audio (auto_split at 7200s). WebRTC VAD is a 6-subband-energy GMM and is the canonical example of why energy features fail on music. inaSpeechSegmenter (CNN, MIREX 2018 winner) is the production speech/music/noise analog but explicitly tags singing voice as 'music' — exactly the distinction we must go beyond. Broadcast music-vs-speech work (CRNN on synthetic mixtures, ICASSP 2021; industrial CNN radio filter, 2025) shows synthetic data augmentation (mixing vocals/instrumentals at varied SNR) is the proven way to train against instrumental-only confusers. None of these handle 'same song, different singer' — that requires a speaker/singer-identity branch on top of the segmenter.

entries.name: Silero VAD v5
entries.key_idea: Pipeline: fixed 512-sample (32ms @16kHz) chunks -> internal STFT -> small conv encoder -> RNN decoder -> per-chunk speech probability, with persistent recurrent state across chunks (model.reset_states() between files). Trained on huge corpora covering 6000+ languages. Sources: https://github.com/snakers4/silero-vad and wiki https://github.com/snakers4/silero-vad/wiki/Performance-Metrics
entries.params_and_speed: JIT model ~2MB (v5; v4 was ~1MB) — so small that quantization gives no speedup. One 31.25ms chunk in 189-325us on 1 CPU thread (Threadripper 3960X): RTS 96 (JIT) to 165 (ONNX), i.e. ~100-165x real-time on a single CPU core; batching/GPU faster still. Negligible GPU memory.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/snakers4/silero-vad (torch.hub / pip silero-vad), MIT license, no telemetry/keys
entries.relevance: The reference design for our smoothing layer: get_speech_timestamps implements threshold=0.5 + neg_threshold=threshold-0.15 (hysteresis), min_speech_duration_ms=250, min_silence_duration_ms=100, speech_pad_ms=30, max_speech_duration_s split at last >100ms silence. We should clone this exact post-processing API for singing segments (with much larger durations, e.g. min_duration_on ~10-30s for songs, min_silence ~3-10s). Also a candidate cheap first-stage gate (any-voice-activity) before the expensive singing/singer classifier. Caveat: it is a speech detector — singing over loud instrumentals is exactly its weak domain, so it cannot be the singing detector itself.

entries.name: pyannote segmentation-3.0 (PyanNet + powerset)
entries.key_idea: PyanNet: learned SincNet bandpass front-end (80 sinc filters, 42.6K params) -> 4-layer BiLSTM (1.4M) -> 2 linear layers -> 7-class softmax over the POWERSET of up to 3 speakers per 10s window (classes: non-speech, spk1, spk2, spk3, spk1+2, spk1+3, spk2+3), one prediction per ~17ms frame (589 frames / 10s). Powerset multi-class cross-entropy replaces per-speaker multi-label sigmoid and removes the detection-threshold hyperparameter (Plaquet & Bredin, Interspeech 2023, https://github.com/FrenchKrab/IS2023-powerset-diarization). Model card: https://huggingface.co/pyannote/segmentation-3.0
entries.params_and_speed: ~1.5M params total; trivially fits any GPU; segmentation step of the diarization pipeline runs many times faster than real time on GPU (the embedding/clustering stages dominate pipeline cost).
entries.pretrained: https://huggingface.co/pyannote/segmentation-3.0 (gated download, MIT license); train/finetune code in https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio
entries.relevance: Closest production analog to our task shape AND the best architectural template: replace its 7 speaker-powerset classes with our confuser-aware classes (e.g. {silence/other, A-speech, music-no-vocals, A-singing, other-singer-singing, A-singing+music...}) — powerset/multiclass forces the model to explicitly separate instrumental-only and other-singer confusers instead of collapsing them into a binary 'singing' label. 1.5M params trains in hours on one 3090. Finetuning recipe exists in pyannote-audio. Its 10s window is still short for 'is this backing track accompanied by live vocals' judgments — we likely want 30s+ windows, which BiLSTM-over-conv-features handles cheaply.

entries.name: pyannote Inference + VoiceActivityDetection pipeline (chunking & smoothing recipe)
entries.key_idea: Long-file handling: apply the model over overlapping sliding windows (training-length chunks, e.g. duration=5-10s, step=10-50% of duration; diarization pipeline uses 90% overlap), then aggregate per-frame scores across windows by overlap-add averaging (Inference.aggregate supports hamming weighting toward chunk centers and warm_up edge-trimming; see pyannote/audio/core/inference.py). Binarization: hysteresis with separate onset/offset thresholds, then min_duration_on (delete short actives) and min_duration_off (fill short gaps). Tutorial: https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio/blob/develop/tutorials/voice_activity_detection.ipynb; hyperparameter semantics paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.04045
entries.params_and_speed: N/A (inference procedure). Overlap factor is the speed knob: 90% overlap = 10x compute; for our throughput budget use step=50-100% of window.
entries.pretrained: pyannote-audio library, MIT, https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio
entries.relevance: This IS our inference design, ready-made: VOD -> overlapping windows -> per-frame P(A-singing) -> overlap-add average -> hysteresis (onset~0.6/offset~0.4) -> min_duration_on/off tuned to song scale -> pad and cut clips. Real tuned values exist in the wild (ByteDance VoxSRC21: onset 0.757, offset 0.727, min_on 0.132s, min_off 0.481s — for speech; ours will be ~100x longer durations). Note: with powerset (segmentation-3.0) onset/offset disappear since argmax replaces thresholding — only min_duration_on/off remain to tune.

entries.name: NVIDIA MarbleNet / Frame-VAD Multilingual MarbleNet v2.0
entries.key_idea: QuartzNet-style deep residual stack of 1D time-channel separable convolutions + BN + ReLU + dropout (MarbleNet-3x2x64: 3 blocks x 2 sub-blocks x 64 channels) over MFCC/log-mel input; matches prior SOTA VAD with ~1/10 the parameters (paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.13886). v2.0 is frame-level: one speech probability per 20ms frame. NeMo splits long inputs with prepare_manifest.auto_split (split_duration=7200s) and post-processes with frame_vad_infer_postprocess.yaml (onset/offset thresholds, pad_onset/pad_offset, min duration filters, median smoothing).
entries.params_and_speed: 91.5K parameters (v2.0, per HF model card) — proof that frame VAD at 20ms resolution needs <0.1M params; far faster than real-time, trivial GPU memory.
entries.pretrained: https://huggingface.co/nvidia/Frame_VAD_Multilingual_MarbleNet_v2.0 (NeMo, EncDecFrameClassificationModel) and NGC https://catalog.ngc.nvidia.com/orgs/nvidia/teams/nemo/models/vad_marblenet/files
entries.relevance: Architecture template if we want a pure-conv (no recurrence) tower: time-channel separable 1D convs give large receptive fields cheaply — directly fixes the old system's 'too small receptive field' problem without BiGRU memory cost, and convs parallelize over the whole VOD on GPU (good for 1-2x real-time on <10GB). Its NeMo postprocessing config (onset/offset/pad_onset/pad_offset/min_duration) is another production smoothing reference. The 7200s auto-split shows how production handles multi-hour files: split at the manifest level, not in the model.

entries.name: TEN VAD
entries.key_idea: Low-latency streaming frame-level VAD from the TEN/Agora framework; operates on 16kHz audio with 10/16ms hop (160/256 samples), includes a pitch-estimation feature (pitch_est.cc derived from LPCNet). Claims higher precision than WebRTC VAD and Silero VAD on a manually annotated testset (LibriSpeech/GigaSpeech/DNS), and much faster speech-to-nonspeech transition detection (Silero lags by hundreds of ms and misses short inter-speech silences). Repo: https://github.com/TEN-framework/ten-vad
entries.params_and_speed: ~86% smaller library and ~32% lower RTF than Silero VAD (so roughly few-hundred-KB ONNX model); CPU real-time many times over; exact param count not published.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/TEN-framework/ten-vad and https://huggingface.co/TEN-framework/ten-vad — CAUTION: Apache 2.0 WITH additional conditions (may not deploy on end-user devices or in ways competing with Agora) — not cleanly permissive like Silero MIT.
entries.relevance: Mostly a benchmark/landscape datapoint: shows the 2025 production frontier for tiny VADs and confirms hop sizes of 10-16ms are standard. Its sharper onset/offset transitions matter for clean clip boundaries, but its restrictive license and speech-only focus make Silero the better off-the-shelf gate for us.

entries.name: WebRTC VAD (py-webrtcvad)
entries.key_idea: Pre-trained two-class GMM over log-energies of 6 sub-bands (80-250, 250-500, 500-1k, 1k-2k, 2k-3k, 3k-4k Hz; input resampled to 8kHz), 10/20/30ms frames, 4 aggressiveness modes that change GMM thresholds (kLocalThreshold/kGlobalThreshold) plus hangover smoothing (kOverHangMax). Sources: https://github.com/wiseman/py-webrtcvad, vad_core.c, and the sub-band analysis at https://cdmana.com/2022/03/202203311224559207.html
entries.params_and_speed: Tiny (a few hundred GMM parameters); essentially free CPU cost; 16-bit mono PCM only.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/wiseman/py-webrtcvad (pip py-webrtcvad); WebRTC BSD-3-Clause
entries.relevance: The canonical negative example and why-not baseline: sub-band energy GMMs cannot distinguish singing from instrumental music — music has high energy across exactly these bands, so it fires on every confuser (a)-(d). Useful only to articulate in DESIGN.md why a learned spectro-temporal model is required. Do not use even as a pre-filter on stream VODs with background music.

entries.name: inaSpeechSegmenter
entries.key_idea: CNN-based segmenter from INA (French National Audiovisual Institute) that splits broadcast audio into homogeneous speech / music / noise zones plus speaker-gender labels; won the MIREX 2018 speech detection challenge and ranked #1 vs 6 open-source VADs on French TV/radio; used in production for the French audiovisual regulator's annual reports. Critical labeling convention: 'speech over music' is tagged speech, and SINGING VOICE IS TAGGED AS MUSIC. Repo: https://github.com/ina-foss/inaSpeechSegmenter
entries.params_and_speed: Small CNN (TensorFlow); processes broadcast archives at scale far faster than real time on GPU; exact params not load-bearing.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/ina-foss/inaSpeechSegmenter (pip inaSpeechSegmenter, MIT license per repo), Docker image inafoss/inaspeechsegmenter
entries.relevance: The closest deployed broadcast analog: long audio -> CSV of labeled segments, exactly our output format. Its 'singing = music' convention pinpoints our gap — production speech/music segmenters deliberately collapse the class we need. Could serve as a coarse pre-segmenter (find 'music' zones = candidate song regions) feeding a second-stage 'is A singing over this music' classifier, cutting compute on multi-hour VODs.

entries.name: CRNN with synthetic data for speech/music detection in radio (Venkatesh et al., ICASSP 2021)
entries.key_idea: Convolutional Recurrent Neural Network trained for frame-level music and speech detection in radio broadcast, trained largely on ARTIFICIALLY SYNTHESISED mixtures (programmatically mixing speech, music, and noise at controlled loudness ratios), outperforming prior SOTA — demonstrating that synthetic mixing solves the scarce-annotation problem for broadcast segmentation. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.09959 (IEEE: https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9413597/). Related: TCN for speech/music detection (ISMIR 2019); comparative studies of CNN/GRU/LSTM/TCN/CRNN for broadcast segmentation; OpenBMAT and MUSAN datasets.
entries.params_and_speed: CRNNs of this class are ~1-5M params, well within budget.
entries.pretrained: No widely-distributed weights; datasets MUSAN (https://www.openslr.org/17/) and OpenBMAT are available for synthesis.
entries.relevance: Directly validates our key training strategy against confusers (a)-(c): synthesize training data by mixing streamer-A vocals (and other-singer vocals) with instrumentals/karaoke tracks at varied SNRs, plus instrumental-only and partial-instrumental negatives. With only one night on 3090s, synthetic mixing from a modest amount of labeled A-singing audio is the highest-leverage data move. CRNN (conv front-end + recurrent context) is also the proven architecture family for exactly this 'music-aware frame labeling' task.

entries.name: Industrial CNN music-vs-non-music discriminator for radio (MDPI Information, 2025)
entries.key_idea: Industrial-grade system distinguishing commercially played songs from non-music in live radio streams, used as a cheap pre-filter before invoking an expensive song-identification service: 139h of radio audio, FM-like augmentation, custom CNN, continuous live-stream inference platform. Paper: https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/16/4/288
entries.params_and_speed: Small custom CNN; runs continuously on live web-radio streams (i.e., comfortably real-time on modest hardware).
entries.pretrained: none (system description paper)
entries.relevance: Production proof of the cascade pattern we should adopt: cheap always-on coarse classifier (music present?) gating an expensive precise stage (is it A singing? singer verification). For AutoSlicer v2: stage-1 tiny frame model finds music/singing candidate regions across the whole VOD fast; stage-2 (larger window, singer-identity-aware) runs only on candidates — keeps whole-stream inference well under the 1-2x real-time and <10GB budget.

entries.name: Powerset multi-class cross-entropy loss (Plaquet & Bredin, Interspeech 2023)
entries.key_idea: Replace per-class multi-label sigmoid outputs with a single softmax over the powerset of label combinations (e.g., all subsets of up-to-2-of-3 speakers). Benefits: no detection threshold hyperparameter (argmax decoding), explicit modeling of co-occurring classes, better accuracy and robustness to domain shift. Official code: https://github.com/FrenchKrab/IS2023-powerset-diarization; deployed in pyannote 3.x.
entries.params_and_speed: Loss/output-head change only; negligible cost.
entries.pretrained: Implemented in pyannote.audio 3.x (MIT); Powerset conversion utility in pyannote.audio.utils.powerset
entries.relevance: Our confusers are literally co-occurrence patterns: {vocals-by-A, vocals-by-other, instrumental-music, speech} can co-occur (A sings over backing track; reference recording plays while A talks). A powerset head over these base events gives the model an explicit 'instrumental-only' class (confusers a-c) and an 'other-singer vocals' class (confuser d), and decoding 'A-singing' = any powerset state containing vocals-by-A. Removes threshold tuning per class; only min_duration smoothing remains.

entries.name: Old AutoSlicer v1 architecture lineage (SincNet front-end + conv tower + BiGRU) vs 2024 practice
entries.key_idea: The v1 design (SincConv stem -> ResNeXt1d 729x downsample -> BiGRU -> per-frame binary) is the same family as PyanNet (SincNet -> BiLSTM), so it was architecturally sound but: (i) binary output cannot represent confusers (fixed by powerset/multiclass), (ii) raw-waveform 8kHz SincNet front-ends have largely been displaced in 2023+ production by log-mel (Silero/MarbleNet/inaSpeechSegmenter all use spectral features) or by frozen SSL features, (iii) receptive-field limits are now solved by either long-window BiLSTM over heavily downsampled conv features (pyannote: 10s) or dilated/separable conv stacks (MarbleNet), with window-level context extended at inference by overlap-add rather than ever-bigger models.
entries.params_and_speed: v1-class models are ~1-5M params; all cited successors fit in <2M and run >>10x real-time on GPU.
entries.pretrained: n/a (analysis entry; sources are the per-system entries above)
entries.relevance: Bridges old system to new design: keep the per-frame labeling + smoothing shape (it is what every production system does), swap binary head for powerset multiclass, swap raw-waveform stem for log-mel (cheaper, robust at 16kHz, lets us precompute features for whole VODs), use 10-30s windows with overlap-add instead of 'minutes of context' inside the model, and add a singer-identity branch (the one component absent from ALL production VADs) for confuser (d).

recommendations:
1) Adopt the universal production shape: log-mel features (16kHz) -> small frame-level network -> per-frame class probabilities -> sliding-window overlap-add -> hysteresis + duration smoothing -> segments. Every system surveyed (Silero, pyannote, MarbleNet, inaSpeechSegmenter, broadcast CNNs) uses exactly this; do not reinvent it.

2) Use a powerset/multiclass output head, not binary. Define base events {A-vocals, other-vocals, instrumental-music, A-speech/other-speech} and train a softmax over their valid combinations (pyannote 3.x powerset, Interspeech 2023). This turns confusers (a)-(c) into an explicit 'instrumental-only' class and confuser (d) into 'other-vocals (+music)', instead of hoping a binary model learns the distinction implicitly. Decoding 'A is singing' = argmax state contains A-vocals; no per-class threshold tuning.

3) Architecture: start by fine-tuning pyannote/segmentation-3.0 (PyanNet, 1.5M params, MIT) with a replaced classification head and longer windows (try 10s baseline, then 30s — BiLSTM over 17ms conv frames makes 30s cheap). Parallel track: a MarbleNet-style time-channel-separable conv tower (~100K-1M params, no recurrence) for maximum GPU throughput — 91.5K params demonstrably suffices for 20ms frame labeling. Both train overnight on one 3090 with room to spare; both infer a multi-hour VOD far faster than real-time in <2GB GPU memory.

4) Confuser (d) (same song, another singer) is NOT solved by any production VAD — it needs singer identity. Add a second branch/stage: pool embeddings (ECAPA/TitaNet-style, or fine-tuned on A's singing voice) over candidate singing frames and verify against enrolled A-singing embeddings. Run it only on stage-1 candidate regions (cascade pattern, as in the 2025 industrial radio system) to stay within the speed budget.

5) Training data: follow the ICASSP 2021 synthetic-mixing result — programmatically mix A's isolated/clean vocals and other singers' vocals with instrumentals/karaoke tracks at varied SNRs and offsets, plus instrumental-only and partial-instrumental negatives (MUSAN and the streamer's own VODs as sources). This directly manufactures all four confusers in arbitrary quantity overnight.

6) Inference plumbing: window = training length, step = 50-100% of window (avoid pyannote's 90% overlap — 10x compute for marginal gain at our segment scale); overlap-add average per-frame probabilities (optionally hamming-weighted). Smoothing at SONG scale: hysteresis onset~0.6/offset~0.4 (skip if powerset), min_duration_on ~ 20-40s (discard blips shorter than a verse), min_duration_off ~ 5-15s (bridge instrumental breaks inside one song so a song isn't split at its bridge/solo), then pad boundaries ~1-2s before cutting clips (Silero speech_pad_ms analog). Tune these four numbers on held-out VODs; they matter as much as the model.

7) Keep Silero VAD (MIT, ~2MB, ~100x real-time on one CPU core) as a free auxiliary input/feature (speech-activity channel helps separate A-talking-over-music from A-singing), but never as the singing detector. Avoid TEN VAD (license restrictions) and WebRTC VAD (energy-GMM, fires on all music).

summary: Research on the "is it really person A singing" sub-problem for AutoSlicer v2. Key verified facts: (1) Off-the-shelf speaker-verification embeddings (ECAPA-TDNN ~6M/14M params, CAM++ ~half ECAPA's FLOPs, WeSpeaker ResNets) are small, Apache-licensed, ONNX-exportable and run far faster than real time, easily fitting the <10GB / 1-2x RT budget. (2) Speech-trained speaker models demonstrably degrade on singing voice: JukeBox (Interspeech 2020) was built specifically to show this, and the ISMIR 2023 Sony CSL "singer identity" paper shows SSL singer encoders trained on isolated vocals outperform speech SV baselines (incl. speaker-verification and wav2vec 2.0 pretrained models) on singing tasks. They still work to a usable degree, but enrollment/fine-tuning on singing data is strongly indicated. (3) Background music is the dominant failure mode: Deezer's ISMIR 2024 cloned-singer-ID paper found mixture-input models deteriorate most, and SingFake (ICASSP 2024) pipelines run Demucs vocal separation before analysis — so vocal separation first is the consensus practice. (4) For cross-domain speech<->singing similarity, SVCC 2023 used a WavLM-Large-based SV model's cosine similarity as the objective metric, and found cross-domain similarity is the hardest axis. (5) Practical recipe: separate vocals, embed 3-5s windows with ECAPA/CAM++ (optionally fine-tuned on streamer A's singing), cosine vs an enrollment centroid built from A's SINGING (not speech), score-normalize against a cohort of other singers, calibrate threshold on a labeled dev set. This directly solves confuser (d) (same song, different singer) because timbre embeddings are content/melody-invariant by construction.

entries.name: ECAPA-TDNN (Desplanques et al.)
entries.key_idea: TDNN x-vector successor with Squeeze-Excitation Res2Net blocks, multi-layer feature aggregation, and channel-dependent attentive statistics pooling; trained with AAM-softmax, verification done by plain cosine distance between 192-d embeddings. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.07143. Remains the default workhorse for speaker verification.
entries.params_and_speed: C=512 version: 5.95M params, EER 1.07%/1.28%/2.43% on Vox1-O/E/H; C=1024 version: 14.4M params, EER ~0.87-0.92% Vox1-O (per https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.17326 and https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.08342). Runs many times faster than real time on CPU, trivially so on GPU; <1GB GPU memory for inference.
entries.pretrained: speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb on HuggingFace, Apache-2.0 license, trained on VoxCeleb1+2 at 16kHz, ~0.8% EER Vox1-O-clean, cosine-similarity verification API built in (https://huggingface.co/speechbrain/spkrec-ecapa-voxceleb). Also WeSpeaker ECAPA512/ECAPA1024 in .pt and ONNX (https://github.com/wenet-e2e/wespeaker/blob/master/docs/pretrained.md, e.g. https://huggingface.co/Wespeaker/wespeaker-ecapa-tdnn512-LM).
entries.relevance: The cheapest credible 'is this person A' verifier. Embed separated-vocal windows, cosine against A's enrollment centroid. Speech-trained, so expect degradation on singing (see JukeBox entry) — mitigate by enrolling on A's singing samples and optionally fine-tuning the last layers on A-vs-others singing data, which fits an RTX 3090 overnight easily at 6M params.

entries.name: CAM++ (Alibaba / 3D-Speaker)
entries.key_idea: Densely-connected TDNN (D-TDNN) backbone with context-aware masking and multi-granularity pooling; explicitly designed because ECAPA's accuracy came with high compute. Narrower but much deeper network. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.00332.
entries.params_and_speed: ~half the parameters and FLOPs of ECAPA-TDNN (~7M vs 14M class), >2x faster inference (RTF measured single-thread CPU) than both ECAPA-TDNN and ResNet34; EER 0.73% VoxCeleb-O, 6.78% CN-Celeb.
entries.pretrained: modelscope/3D-Speaker toolkit (https://github.com/modelscope/3D-Speaker, Apache-2.0 repo) with CAM++ recipes and released embedding models incl. a Chinese model trained on ~200k speakers and an English VoxCeleb model; ONNX Runtime inference scripts released April 2024.
entries.relevance: Best accuracy-per-FLOP option for scanning multi-hour VODs. The 200k-speaker Chinese pretrained CAM++ is notable if streamer A sings in Chinese/Japanese-adjacent content — much broader speaker coverage than VoxCeleb. Drop-in replacement for ECAPA in the cosine-verification pipeline.

entries.name: WeSpeaker toolkit (wenet-e2e)
entries.key_idea: Production-oriented speaker embedding toolkit: ResNet34/152/221/293 r-vectors, CAM++, ECAPA, large-margin fine-tuning (LM) for long audio, AS-Norm score normalization, ONNX/TensorRT/Triton export. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.17016; repo: https://github.com/wenet-e2e/wespeaker.
entries.params_and_speed: ResNet34: 0.723% EER Vox1-O (after LM + AS-Norm), ~6.6M params; ResNet293_LM: 0.447% EER (bigger, ~30M+); all exportable to ONNX with a one-line infer_onnx.py path.
entries.pretrained: Pretrained .pt and ONNX checkpoints for ResNet34/152/221/293, CAM++, ECAPA512 on the repo's pretrained.md and HuggingFace (Wespeaker org). Repo Apache-2.0.
entries.relevance: If you want to fine-tune a verifier on A-vs-other-singers tonight, WeSpeaker gives you the training recipes (AAM, LM fine-tune) plus built-in AS-Norm — which is exactly the score-normalization machinery you need for robust cosine thresholding across varying songs/mics. LM-finetuned models are specifically better on >3s segments, matching your 3-10s analysis windows.

entries.name: WavLM-Large + ECAPA head (Microsoft)
entries.key_idea: Self-supervised 24-layer transformer pretrained on 94k hours; frame features feed an ECAPA-TDNN head producing 192-d speaker embeddings. SOTA speaker verification and — importantly — the model SVCC 2023 chose as its objective cross-domain speaker-similarity judge. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.13900.
entries.params_and_speed: 316M (WavLM-Large) + ~4.8M (ECAPA head) ≈ 321M params; EER 0.383%/0.480%/0.986% Vox1-O/E/H with LM fine-tune + calibration. Roughly ~60 GFLOPs/sec of audio — still real-time-capable on a 3090 and fits in <10GB at fp16, but ~20-50x more compute than ECAPA.
entries.pretrained: microsoft/wavlm-large and SV-finetuned variants on HuggingFace (microsoft/unilm repo, MIT-licensed code); ESPnet-SPK reproduction at 0.39% EER.
entries.relevance: Use as the high-accuracy second-stage verifier or offline labeler: run cheap ECAPA/CAM++ over the whole VOD, then re-verify candidate song segments with WavLM-SV. SVCC 2023 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.14422) validated exactly this model for speech-vs-singing speaker similarity scoring, making it the best-evidenced cross-domain choice.

entries.name: JukeBox: A Multilingual Singer Recognition Dataset (Chowdhury, Cozzo, Ross)
entries.key_idea: 467 hours / 936 singers of singing audio (16kHz) with identity, gender, language labels, built specifically to test speaker recognition on singing. Demonstrates that state-of-the-art speaker models trained on spoken voice alone perform poorly on singing voice (xVector-PLDA baselines trained on VoxCeleb2 vs JukeBox vs both). Paper: https://www.isca-archive.org/interspeech_2020/chowdhury20b_interspeech.pdf, https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.03507.
entries.params_and_speed: Dataset, not a model.
entries.pretrained: Dataset at http://iprobe.cse.msu.edu/datasets/jukebox.html (research license).
entries.relevance: The load-bearing classic answering your core question: a speech-trained speaker model does NOT transfer cleanly to singing — pitch range, vibrato, and vocal effort shift the embedding distribution. Direct implication for AutoSlicer: enroll person A from her SINGING clips (not talking), and if fine-tuning, mix singing data in. JukeBox itself is usable as fine-tuning/cohort data.

entries.name: Singer Identity Representation Learning using Self-Supervised Techniques (Torres, Lattner, Richard — ISMIR 2023)
entries.key_idea: Trains singer-identity encoders with SSL (SimCLR/VICReg/BYOL/uniformity-alignment) on a large corpus of ISOLATED vocal tracks at 44.1kHz, with pitch/content-invariance augmentations. Evaluated on singer similarity + identification with out-of-domain tests; their singing-trained encoders outperform both speaker-verification and wav2vec 2.0 speech-pretrained baselines on singing voice. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05064, https://archives.ismir.net/ismir2023/paper/000053.pdf.
entries.params_and_speed: Lightweight conv encoder operating at 44.1kHz (exact param count not confirmed in this research pass; comparable to EfficientNet-class encoders, well under 3090 limits).
entries.pretrained: Code + released models at https://github.com/SonyCSLParis/ssl-singer-identity (check repo license before commercial use).
entries.relevance: Second key evidence point that speech SV baselines are beatable on singing, and a directly reusable singing-specialized embedding. Strong candidate to ensemble with ECAPA: ECAPA covers A's speech/timbre identity, this covers singing-specific timbre. It also operates on separated vocals — consistent with a separation-first pipeline.

entries.name: From Real to Cloned Singer Identification (Deezer — ISMIR 2024)
entries.key_idea: Singer-level contrastive learning (positives = vocal segments of same singer) trained at industrial scale (millions of tracks); three variants taking mixtures, separated vocals, or both as input. Mixture-input models degrade most on out-of-distribution (cloned) voices and on vocals over unfamiliar instrumentals; genres with heavy vocal effects are hardest. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08647.
entries.params_and_speed: Transformer-based audio encoder (foundation/ dir in repo); sized for large-scale training, inference well within a 3090.
entries.pretrained: Code at https://github.com/deezer/real-cloned-singer-id (training/eval code + FMA/MTG splits in Releases); their internal weights/datasets NOT released.
entries.relevance: Two transferable lessons: (1) vocal-stem input beats mixture input for robust singer identity — supports running Demucs/MDX separation before embedding; (2) singer-level contrastive fine-tuning is the right objective if you train your own A-vs-world verifier head tonight. Also directly informs confuser (d): identity models keyed on timbre, not song content, are what distinguish A from another person singing the same song.

entries.name: Singer ID on artist20: CRNN / KNN-Net line of work
entries.key_idea: Classic polyphonic singer-ID benchmark: artist20 = 20 artists / 1413 songs. CRNN baseline reaches F1 ~0.81; KNN-Net (attention-CRNN with a KNN output layer for interpretable timbre matching) reaches ~85% accuracy (https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.10236, https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9413774/). Sharma et al. 2019 showed Wave-U-Net vocal separation + i-vector singer modeling improves polyphonic singer ID — early evidence for separation-first.
entries.params_and_speed: CRNN/KNN-Net are small (<10M) models; trivial inference cost.
entries.pretrained: Mostly research code, no canonical maintained weights; artist20 dataset is the reusable asset. Survey: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13636-024-00336-8.
entries.relevance: Calibrates expectations: even closed-set 20-way singer ID on full mixtures plateaus around 81-85% — below your >90% target — whereas verification on SEPARATED vocals with enrollment data is an easier, better-conditioned problem. Reinforces: separate vocals, do verification (1-vs-all) not classification.

entries.name: SingFake / SVDD Challenge (Zang, Zhang, Heydari, Duan — ICASSP 2024)
entries.key_idea: First in-the-wild singing-voice deepfake dataset (28.9h bonafide + 29.4h fake, 40 singers, 5 languages); pipeline uses Demucs to separate vocals before clip generation. Shows speech-trained anti-spoofing systems degrade significantly on singing and that background music hides artifacts; retraining on singing (separated vocals or mixtures) substantially recovers performance. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07525, site: https://singfake.org, code: https://github.com/yongyizang/SingFake.
entries.params_and_speed: Dataset + benchmark of standard countermeasure models (AASIST-class, <1M-30M params).
entries.pretrained: Dataset via singfake.org; repo has Demucs separation + VAD + splitting scripts you can reuse verbatim.
entries.relevance: Relevant to confuser (d) in its strongest form (a played-back recording or AI cover of the same song by another voice): combines with speaker verification as a 'is this a real live A' check. Also a third independent confirmation of the speech-to-singing domain gap and of Demucs-separation-first as standard practice. Their separate.py/split.py preprocessing scripts map directly onto your VOD pipeline.

entries.name: Singing Voice Conversion Challenge 2023 (SVCC 2023)
entries.key_idea: Benchmark establishing how speaker similarity is measured across the speech/singing domain boundary: objective speaker similarity = cosine similarity of embeddings from a WavLM-Large-based speaker verification model; subjective 4-point same-speaker tests as ground truth. Findings: cross-domain (speech-enrolled target, singing output) similarity is much harder than in-domain, no system matched true-target similarity, and only a few objective metrics correlated significantly with perception. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.14422; site: https://vc-challenge.org/svcc2023/index.html.
entries.params_and_speed: Evaluation protocol, not a model (WavLM-Large SV: ~321M params, see WavLM entry).
entries.pretrained: n/a (protocol); the WavLM SV checkpoint it used is public on HuggingFace.
entries.relevance: Field-standard precedent for your exact mechanism — cosine similarity of SV embeddings on singing voice — plus the caveat that raw cosine across speech/singing domains is noisy. Practical takeaways: enroll A with singing references (avoid the cross-domain penalty entirely), and validate your threshold against human-labeled segments rather than trusting absolute cosine values.

entries.name: Resemblyzer (GE2E voice encoder, resemble-ai)
entries.key_idea: Pip-installable GE2E-loss LSTM voice encoder producing 256-d utterance embeddings; pretrained model from Real-Time-Voice-Cloning. Designed for quick speaker similarity, diarization, and fake-speech screening demos. Repo: https://github.com/resemble-ai/Resemblyzer (Apache-2.0).
entries.params_and_speed: ~1.4M params (3-layer LSTM-256); ~1000x real time on a GTX 1080; CPU-friendly.
entries.pretrained: Bundled with `pip install resemblyzer`, Apache-2.0.
entries.relevance: Useful as a 30-minute baseline to validate the whole pipeline (enroll A, scan VOD, plot cosine traces) before committing to ECAPA/CAM++. Accuracy is well below modern models (2017-era GE2E), so treat as scaffolding, not the final verifier.

entries.name: ERes2Net / 3D-Speaker-Toolkit (ModelScope)
entries.key_idea: Open toolkit covering supervised (ECAPA, ResNet34, Res2Net, ERes2Net, ERes2NetV2, CAM++) and self-supervised (DINO, RDINO, SDPN) speaker models, plus diarization; ERes2Net adds local/global feature fusion to Res2Net for stronger small-model performance. Toolkit paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.19971; repo: https://github.com/modelscope/3D-Speaker.
entries.params_and_speed: ERes2Net base ~6-7M params, EER competitive with ECAPA-1024 at half the size; ONNX Runtime inference scripts available.
entries.pretrained: ModelScope + HuggingFace releases (e.g. iic/speech_campplus_sv_zh-cn_16k-common 200k-speaker model; iic/speech_eres2net_sv_en_voxceleb_16k). Repo Apache-2.0; check individual model cards.
entries.relevance: One-stop alternative to WeSpeaker with strong small models and very large speaker-coverage Chinese checkpoints; ERes2Net is a good third candidate alongside ECAPA-512 and CAM++ for the per-window verifier under your compute budget.

recommendations:
1) Architecture for the 'is it A singing' verifier: vocal separation -> windowed speaker embedding -> cosine vs enrollment centroid. Run HTDemucs/MDX vocal separation on candidate singing regions (gated by your singing-voice-detection head, NOT the whole VOD, to stay under 1-2x RT), resample stems to 16kHz, embed 3-5s windows (50% hop) with ECAPA-TDNN-512 (speechbrain, Apache-2.0, 6M params) or CAM++ (3D-Speaker, ~2x faster), and compare to a centroid built from 1-3 minutes of person A's SEPARATED SINGING vocals (multiple songs, multiple sessions). Three independent sources (JukeBox, SingFake, Deezer ISMIR 2024) show mixtures/background music are the main failure mode and separation-first is standard practice — do not skip separation for the verification stage.

2) Enroll on singing, not speech. JukeBox and SVCC 2023 both establish a real speech<->singing domain gap for speaker models. Avoid it entirely by enrolling A from her own singing clips. If you have ~30+ labeled songs of A plus negatives (other streamers' singing, JukeBox/OpenSinger/M4Singer singers), spend one 3090-night fine-tuning ECAPA with AAM-softmax or singer-level contrastive loss (WeSpeaker recipes give you LM fine-tuning + AS-Norm out of the box) — this is the single highest-leverage training run for pushing past 90%.

3) Thresholding: don't use a fixed raw cosine. Use AS-Norm (cohort = embeddings of many non-A singers' separated vocals, available in WeSpeaker) or at minimum per-VOD z-normalization, then pick the operating threshold on a labeled dev set of A-singing vs confusers; SVCC 2023 found absolute cosine values correlate only loosely with perceptual identity. Aggregate per-window cosines over a candidate segment (median or top-k mean) before thresholding — segment-level decisions are far more reliable than per-window ones.

4) Confuser handling: (a)/(b)/(c) pure or partial instrumentals are handled upstream by the singing-vocal-detection head — additionally, the separated vocal stem of an instrumental is near-silent, so an energy gate on the stem is a free second check. (d) another singer's recording of the same song is exactly what speaker verification solves: timbre embeddings are content/melody-invariant, so A's enrollment centroid rejects other voices regardless of song identity. For the hardest variant of (d) — an AI cover cloned to sound like A — a verifier alone is insufficient (Deezer 2024 shows clones partially fool singer-ID); if that matters, borrow SingFake/SVDD-style countermeasures, but for ordinary reference recordings cosine verification suffices.

5) Compute fit: ECAPA-512/CAM++ inference is negligible (<1GB GPU, >>10x RT); WavLM-Large-SV (~321M, the SVCC-2023-validated judge) fits in <10GB fp16 and is worth using as a second-pass re-verifier on the handful of candidate segments per VOD, keeping the cheap model for the full-stream scan. Separation is your compute budget item — schedule it only inside detected-singing regions. Optionally ensemble the Sony CSL ssl-singer-identity encoder (singing-specialized, 44.1kHz, pretrained weights on GitHub) with ECAPA; the ISMIR 2023 results suggest it adds complementary singing-timbre information that speech-trained models miss.

summary: Research on turning per-frame singing probabilities into clean song-level segments, plus long-context temporal modeling at ~1Hz. Key verified findings: (1) SED post-processing is a first-class lever — class-dependent smoothing + double-threshold/hysteresis was shown to change event-F1 dramatically (Cances et al. 2019), and the 2024 SOTA move is change-point-based Sound Event Bounding Boxes (cSEBBs, Interspeech 2024, MERL code), which decouples boundary placement from confidence thresholding and lifted DCASE 2023 SOTA PSDS1 from .644 to .686/.703. (2) Evaluation: sed_eval (segment/event F1 with collars), psds_eval (PSDS, threshold-free intersection-based), sed_scores_eval (exact all-threshold curves) are the standard toolkits; DCASE wisdom is that frame-F1-optimal and event-F1-optimal post-processing differ (event metrics reward longer median filters / min-duration / gap-fill), so tune post-processing against the deployment metric (event-level F1 with generous collars for song clipping). (3) pyannote's VAD pipeline (onset/offset hysteresis, min_duration_on/off, pad_onset/offset) is a direct, battle-tested template for the segment-builder. (4) For long context at ~1Hz frame rate, a 3-4h VOD is only ~10-15k frames, so dilated TCN (MS-TCN-style), a small Conformer (10M-param class), or bidirectional Mamba (Audio Mamba / SELD-Mamba, linear complexity) over pooled per-second embeddings all integrate minutes of context for negligible GPU cost; the cheapest is a dilated-conv TCN second-stage. (5) Music-structure tools (All-In-One analyzer, MIT, demixed-audio + dilated neighborhood attention) can refine cut points to musical boundaries and split medleys. Note: several web searches were blocked by transient API errors; load-bearing claims were verified by fetching arXiv abstracts and GitHub repo metadata directly via curl.

entries.name: Cances et al., 'Evaluation of post-processing algorithms for polyphonic sound event detection'
entries.key_idea: Systematic study (on DCASE 2018 Task 4) of the segmentation step that turns frame-level class probability curves into events: smoothing (median/mean filters), absolute thresholding vs double-threshold (hysteresis: high threshold to trigger an event, low threshold to extend/close it), and per-class ('class-dependent parametric') optimization of these hyperparameters. Showed post-processing choice has a crucial impact on final event-based F-score, with class-dependent parametric methods best. Verified abstract: https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.06909
entries.params_and_speed: Post-processing only; negligible compute (1-D filtering + thresholding on probability curves).
entries.pretrained: none (algorithmic recipe; trivially reimplemented)
entries.relevance: Direct blueprint for AutoSlicer v2's segmenter: hysteresis double-threshold + smoothing tuned per metric is the standard first thing to do with the per-frame 'A-is-singing' probability before any fancier model. Class-dependent tuning generalizes to tuning per output head (singing vs instrumental-only).

entries.name: Sound Event Bounding Boxes (cSEBBs) — Ebbers et al., Interspeech 2024 (MERL)
entries.key_idea: Shows frame-level thresholding couples event-extent estimation to confidence, degrading onset/offset accuracy. Proposes predicting each event as a tuple (class, extent, overall confidence) — a 1-D bounding box — and a change-detection algorithm (cSEBBs: step-filter change-point detection + segment merging) that converts ANY legacy frame-level probability output into such boxes; final decisions are event-level thresholds on box confidence. Boosted DCASE 2023 SOTA from .644 to .686 PSDS1 (.703 hybrid), and the DCASE 2024 Task 4 winner used cSEBBs post-processing. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.04212 (verified abstract); also ISCA archive ebbers24_interspeech.
entries.params_and_speed: Pure post-processing on 1-D score curves; CPU, effectively free at inference. Includes csebbs.tune() for hyperparameter search (filter lengths, merge thresholds).
entries.pretrained: Code: https://github.com/merlresearch/sebbs (works on sed_scores_eval-format score files)
entries.relevance: State-of-the-art replacement for median-filter+threshold: gives clean song boundaries whose placement does not shift as we tune sensitivity — exactly what we need so a low-confidence instrumental bridge doesn't truncate a song clip. Drop-in on top of any per-frame model we train tonight.

entries.name: sed_eval toolbox / 'Metrics for Polyphonic Sound Event Detection' (Mesaros, Heittola, Virtanen)
entries.key_idea: Defines the standard segment-based and event-based metrics: segment-based F1/error-rate computed in fixed time grids (frame-F1 generalization), and event-based F1 where a prediction counts as TP only if onset (and optionally offset) fall within a collar (commonly 200 ms; collar size is configurable). The DCASE community's long-standing finding: systems/post-processing optimal for segment(frame)-F1 are NOT optimal for event-F1 — event metrics reward temporal smoothing, minimum-duration filtering and gap-filling that merge fragmented detections, while frame metrics reward raw per-frame accuracy.
entries.params_and_speed: Evaluation library only (Python).
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/TUT-ARG/sed_eval — MIT license, 161 stars (verified via GitHub API). Paper: Applied Sciences 6(6):162, 2016.
entries.relevance: For song clipping, the deployment metric is event-level: 'did we emit one clip per song with boundaries within a few seconds'. Use sed_eval event-based F1 with a generous collar (e.g. 5-10 s, t-collar parameter) as the tuning target for all post-processing, not frame accuracy.

entries.name: PSDS / psds_eval (Bilen et al., ICASSP 2020, Audio Analytic)
entries.key_idea: Polyphonic Sound Detection Score: intersection-based (DTC/GTC ratio) matching instead of strict collars — a single detection covering two close ground-truth events is not penalized — and evaluation as area under a PSD-ROC across operating points, decoupling model quality from threshold tuning. Standard metric of DCASE Task 4 since 2021 (PSDS1 = localization-focused, PSDS2 = tagging-focused). Source: https://www.audioanalytic.com/psds-polyphonic-sound-detection-score/ ; maintained forks https://github.com/j-bernardi/psds_eval and DCASE-REPO.
entries.params_and_speed: Evaluation library only.
entries.pretrained: psds_eval on GitHub, MIT license (per Audio Analytic announcement).
entries.relevance: Intersection-based matching (e.g. require detection to cover >=70% of the true song and be >=70% inside it) is a far better fit for 3-minute song events than 200 ms collars; use a PSDS-style intersection criterion for model selection, then pick one operating point for production.

entries.name: sed_scores_eval — 'Threshold Independent Evaluation of SED Scores' (Ebbers et al., ICASSP 2022)
entries.key_idea: Computes PSD-ROC and collar/intersection-based performance curves EXACTLY over all possible decision thresholds jointly (not a sampled threshold grid, which can severely bias PSDS), and lets you select the threshold that best fulfills a given application requirement. Verified abstract: https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.13148
entries.params_and_speed: Evaluation library; fast enough for per-epoch validation.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/fgnt/sed_scores_eval — MIT license (verified via GitHub API). Also the required input format for merlresearch/sebbs.
entries.relevance: Use during tonight's training to pick the operating point (onset/offset thresholds) on the dev VODs without hand-sweeping thresholds; integrates directly with cSEBBs tuning.

entries.name: pyannote.audio VAD pipeline (hysteresis + duration heuristics)
entries.key_idea: Production-grade recipe for binarizing frame probabilities, with exactly four interpretable knobs: onset threshold (start segment when prob rises above it), offset threshold (end when prob falls below it — hysteresis prevents flicker), min_duration_on (delete segments shorter than X s), min_duration_off (fill gaps shorter than Y s), plus pad_onset/pad_offset for boundary padding. Tuned examples in the wild: ByteDance VoxCeleb 2021 used onset=0.767, offset=0.713, min_duration_on=0.182 s, min_duration_off=0.501 s. Sources: https://github.com/pyannote/pyannote-audio/blob/main/tutorials/voice_activity_detection.ipynb , https://huggingface.co/pyannote/segmentation
entries.params_and_speed: Heuristic post-processing, CPU-trivial. (The pyannote segmentation model itself is ~1.5M-param PyanNet but we only need the pipeline logic.)
entries.pretrained: pyannote-audio on GitHub, MIT license; pyannote/segmentation weights on HF (gated, MIT).
entries.relevance: This is the exact algorithm shape AutoSlicer needs, just with song-scale constants: hysteresis thresholds ~0.5/0.35, min_duration_on ~45-60 s (minimum song length), min_duration_off ~20-40 s (so instrumental solos/bridges with no singing stay inside one song clip), pad ~2-5 s each side before cutting.

entries.name: MS-TCN: Multi-Stage Temporal Convolutional Network (Farha & Gall, CVPR 2019)
entries.key_idea: Multi-stage stack of dilated 1-D convolutions (dilation doubling per layer gives exponentially large receptive field — minutes of context with ~10 layers at 1 Hz) where each stage refines the previous stage's frame-wise predictions; trained with classification loss + a truncated-MSE smoothing loss on log-prob differences that explicitly penalizes over-segmentation. The canonical 'learned post-processing' for long untrimmed sequences in action segmentation. Verified abstract: https://arxiv.org/abs/1903.01945
entries.params_and_speed: Original ~0.8M params per stage (4 stages, ~3M total); on 1 Hz features a full 4 h VOD is one forward pass of a tiny conv net — far under 10 GB, >>100x real-time.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/yabufarha/ms-tcn (research code; trivially reimplemented for 1-D audio features). No audio weights — train tonight on our labels.
entries.relevance: Cheapest learned way to integrate minutes of context: run a frozen per-second embedding/probability extractor, then a 2-4 stage dilated TCN over the whole VOD that jointly smooths and refines. The smoothing loss directly optimizes against fragmented song detections (the failure mode of the 2021 BiGRU system).

entries.name: Conformer (Gulati et al., 2020) — small/streaming variants
entries.key_idea: Convolution-augmented Transformer: self-attention for global context + depthwise conv module for local patterns. The paper's small model is only 10M params with competitive WER (verified abstract: https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.08100). For multi-hour inputs, chunked/streaming attention variants (block-wise attention with memory, e.g. Emformer-style) bound memory; but at ~1 Hz frame rate a 4 h stream is only ~14,400 tokens, so even full attention with a 4-6 layer Conformer-lite is feasible on <10 GB.
entries.params_and_speed: 10M-param class; on 1 Hz pooled embeddings, attention over 14k frames x small dim runs in seconds on a 3090, easily <10 GB with chunking.
entries.pretrained: Architectures in ESPnet/NeMo/torchaudio (Apache-2.0); train the temporal head ourselves.
entries.relevance: Mid-cost option for the second-stage temporal model if dilated TCN underfits: attention lets the model relate a frame to the song's start (e.g. 'backing track started 90 s ago and singing was present at the start') — useful against confusers (b)/(c) partial-instrumental cases.

entries.name: Audio Mamba (AuM) + Mamba-for-SED line (SELD-Mamba, BiMamba-PSELDnet)
entries.key_idea: AuM: first self-attention-free, purely bidirectional-SSM audio classifier; linear time/memory in sequence length, matches or beats AST across six benchmarks (paper https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.03344, code https://github.com/kaistmm/Audio-Mamba-AuM, BSD-3-Clause, verified via GitHub API). Follow-ons replace Conformer blocks with bidirectional Mamba in event-detection systems: SELD-Mamba (EINV2 + BiMamba, DCASE 2024 Task 3, https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.05057) and stereo PSELDnet+BiMamba (DCASE 2025, code https://github.com/alexandergwm/DCASE2025_TASK3_Stereo_PSELD_Mamba) report Transformer-level accuracy with lower compute.
entries.params_and_speed: AuM comes in Tiny/Small/Base sizes mirroring ViM/AST scales (Base comparable to AST-B ~86M; Tiny/Small are a few to tens of M); linear-complexity scan makes hour-long sequences tractable; needs causal-conv CUDA kernels (mamba-ssm) — fine on 3090.
entries.pretrained: AuM AudioSet/VGGSound checkpoints in the kaistmm repo (BSD-3-Clause).
entries.relevance: If we want ONE end-to-end long-context model instead of a two-stage pipeline, a BiMamba temporal tower over per-second features handles unbounded VOD length at linear cost. Realistically optional for tonight: at 1 Hz the sequence is short enough that TCN/Conformer suffice; Mamba is the hedge if we move to 10-25 Hz frames.

entries.name: All-In-One Music Structure Analyzer (Taejun Kim & Juhan Nam)
entries.key_idea: Single model that jointly does beat/downbeat tracking + functional structure segmentation/labeling (intro/verse/chorus/outro) on source-separated (demixed) spectrograms, using dilated neighborhood attention for long-range temporal dependencies; SOTA on HarmonixSet with relatively few parameters. Verified abstract: https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.16425 (WASPAA 2023).
entries.params_and_speed: Lightweight vs prior SOTA (uses NATTEN dilated neighborhood attention); runs on a few-minute song in seconds on GPU; designed for song-length inputs, not whole VODs.
entries.pretrained: https://github.com/mir-aidj/all-in-one — MIT license, 782 stars (verified via GitHub API); pip-installable 'allin1' with pretrained weights.
entries.relevance: Use as a boundary REFINER, not detector: after coarse song segments are found, run all-in-one (or simple SSM-novelty thumbnailing à la Müller's fitness/self-similarity approach) on the segment ± 30 s to snap cut points to true musical start/end, split back-to-back songs in medleys, and confirm a segment has verse/chorus structure (rejecting random humming).

entries.name: Karaoke/clip-pipeline heuristics (SingFake pipeline, karaok-AI, community auto-clippers)
entries.key_idea: Practical pipelines for cutting sung clips from long recordings converge on the same recipe: (1) source-separate vocals (Demucs/Spleeter) so detection runs on the vocal stem; (2) run VAD/SVD on the stem to get timecodes; (3) merge gaps shorter than N seconds, drop segments under a minimum song length, pad boundaries; (4) cut with ffmpeg. SingFake's data pipeline (separate.py -> VAD timecodes -> split.py) is a documented instance: https://github.com/yongyizang/SingFake ; karaok-AI does automatic clip creation from vocals+lyrics extraction: https://github.com/EtienneAb3d/karaok-AI
entries.params_and_speed: Demucs htdemucs ~80M params, roughly real-time-or-faster on GPU per stream chunk; heuristic stage is CPU-trivial.
entries.pretrained: SingFake repo (research license, check repo); Demucs (MIT) / Spleeter (MIT) weights public.
entries.relevance: Confirms the heuristic constants others ship: gap-merge and min-length are the two highest-leverage knobs. Crucially, running detection on the SEPARATED VOCAL stem of the stream mix also gives a clean handle on confusers: pure instrumental (a)-(c) has near-silent vocal stem; confuser (d) (another singer's recording) survives separation, so it must be handled by the speaker/singer-ID head, not by VAD.

entries.name: HMM/Viterbi decoding as segment smoother (classic SED/VAD practice)
entries.year: classic (1990s speech VAD onward; used in DCASE baselines pre-2018)
entries.key_idea: Model the per-frame probability sequence with a 2-state (singing / not-singing) HMM whose transition probabilities encode expected durations, and Viterbi-decode the globally optimal state path. Equivalent to an optimal version of min-duration + gap-fill: a low transition probability makes short flips cost more than they gain. Duration-explicit variants (HSMM) encode 'songs last 1-6 min' directly. This is the principled alternative to median filtering, and unlike median filters its smoothing strength is asymmetric and tunable per transition (entering vs leaving a song).
entries.params_and_speed: 4 transition params; Viterbi on a 14k-frame, 2-state chain is microseconds (e.g. librosa.sequence.viterbi_discriminative).
entries.pretrained: none needed; available in librosa/hmmlearn (ISC/BSD).
entries.relevance: A 30-second implementation tonight that fixes the 2021 system's flicker: set P(stay-in-song) high enough that a 30 s instrumental bridge with p~0.3 doesn't break the segment, while a 2 s vocal shout between songs doesn't start one. Compare head-to-head with median filter and cSEBBs using event-F1 at 5 s collar.

recommendations: Concrete design for AutoSlicer v2's segment stage: (1) Architecture: run the per-frame singing/target-singer model at a coarse output rate (~1 Hz, possibly on the Demucs vocal stem), then add a SECOND-STAGE temporal model over the whole VOD's per-second embeddings — start with a 2-3 stage dilated TCN (MS-TCN-style, ~1-3M params, receptive field 10+ minutes, trains in minutes on a 3090, inference is hundreds-x real-time on <2 GB) with MS-TCN's truncated-MSE smoothing loss to kill over-segmentation; a 4-6 layer Conformer-lite (10M class) or BiMamba head are the upgrades if TCN underfits, since a 4 h VOD at 1 Hz is only ~14.4k tokens — full attention is already affordable, so exotic streaming attention is unnecessary at this frame rate. (2) Post-processing (do this first, it is the cheapest accuracy win): hysteresis double-threshold (onset≈0.5-0.6, offset≈0.3-0.4) -> Viterbi 2-state smoothing or cSEBBs change-point conversion (github.com/merlresearch/sebbs, drop-in) -> min_duration_off (gap-fill) ≈ 20-40 s so instrumental solos/bridges stay inside one clip -> min_duration_on ≈ 45-60 s minimum song length -> pad ±2-5 s; expose all five constants as config and tune them on dev VODs with sed_scores_eval. Important interaction: large gap-fill is what makes confusers (a)-(c) dangerous (a backing track playing before/after singing gets merged in) — gate gap-filling on a second 'matching backing track continues' or music-on signal, or fill gaps only when flanked by high-confidence singing of the same song. (3) Metrics: do NOT model-select on frame-F1; use event-based F1 (sed_eval) with a song-appropriate collar (5-10 s) AND a PSDS-style intersection criterion (detection covers >=70% of song, >=70% inside) via psds_eval/sed_scores_eval — DCASE experience says post-processing optima differ per metric, and our product metric is 'one clip per song, boundaries within seconds'. (4) Boundary refinement: snap final cut points to musical boundaries using the All-In-One structure analyzer (pip allin1, MIT) or simple self-similarity novelty on the segment ±30 s; this also splits medleys/back-to-back songs that gap-fill would merge. (5) Confuser (d) (recording of another singer) cannot be solved by any temporal post-processing — it must be handled upstream by the singer-ID/target-speaker head; the temporal stage should consume BOTH probabilities (any-singing, is-singer-A) so Viterbi/TCN can use 'music continues but singer-A confidence collapsed' as a boundary cue. Total added inference cost of everything above is <5% of the embedding extractor; well within the <10 GB, 1-2x real-time budget.

summary: Music source separation is mature enough to use as an offline data factory and (if desired) even as an online feature extractor within your speed budget. Three tiers exist: (1) fast/light MDX-Net ONNX models (~63MB files, UVR ecosystem) and HTDemucs (~42M params, MIT, 3-7GB VRAM, `--two-stems=vocals`) that run many times faster than real time on a 3090-class GPU; (2) SOTA-quality BS-RoFormer / Mel-RoFormer (SDX23 MSS winner, 72-93M params per stem) that are several times slower but still comfortably faster than real time on a 3090 for overnight pseudo-labeling — community checkpoints (viperx BS-RoFormer ep317, KimberleyJensen Mel-RoFormer, vocal SDR ~10.9-11.0 on the Multisong benchmark) are freely downloadable via TRvlvr/model_repo and the ZFTurbo MIT training framework; (3) classical HPSS / band-energy methods that are nearly free but cannot isolate vocals (vocals land in the harmonic component together with guitars/synths), so they fail exactly on your confusers (a)-(d). Concrete verified speed datapoints: Demucs CPU ~1.5x track duration; T4 GPU ~96s per ~4-min track; TensorRT 6-stem HTDemucs ~5s/song on RTX 3090 (~35x RT); MLX port 34x RT on M4 Max; BS-RoFormer ~0.13s per chunk on RTX 4090 (PyTorch; TensorRT was 2x SLOWER), and on an M3 Max a 3-min song took 1m49s (BS-RoFormer) vs 36s (MDX-Net Inst_HQ_4) vs 19s (VR-arch 2_HP). For a 6-hour VOD on a 3090, even PyTorch HTDemucs 2-stem separation finishes in tens of minutes, i.e. well inside a 1-2x-RT total budget — but the better architecture is to spend separation offline (instrumental-only negatives, other-singer + same-accompaniment hard negatives, vocal-stem speaker embeddings) and distill a "vocal energy" target into the detector so inference needs no separator at all.

entries.name: HTDemucs (Demucs v4, Hybrid Transformer Demucs)
entries.key_idea: Hybrid temporal+spectral bi-U-Net whose innermost layers are replaced by a cross-domain Transformer encoder (self-attention within each domain, cross-attention across waveform/spectrogram branches). 9.00 dB SDR on MUSDB-HQ (9.20 with fine-tuning/sparse attention). Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.08553 ; repo: https://github.com/facebookresearch/demucs . Has a built-in 2-stem mode: `demucs --two-stems=vocals song.mp3` outputs vocals + accompaniment only. Note: HT models support max segment length 7.8s internally (chunked inference handles long files).
entries.params_and_speed: ~41-42M params (per DTTNet and HS-TasNet benchmark tables). GPU memory: >=3GB with --segment 8, ~7GB with default args (official README). Speed: CPU ~1.5x track duration; Replicate T4 ~96s typical per track; TensorRT-optimized 6-stem build ~5s per 3-min song on RTX 3090 (~35x real-time); MLX port 34x RT on M4 Max. htdemucs_ft (fine-tuned bag) is 4x slower for slightly better quality. Plain PyTorch on a 3090 sits comfortably >10x real-time for 2-stem.
entries.pretrained: Official weights auto-downloaded by the demucs package (htdemucs, htdemucs_ft, htdemucs_6s, mdx_extra). Code MIT license (verified via GitHub API). https://github.com/facebookresearch/demucs
entries.relevance: Best speed/quality/ease tradeoff for the offline data factory: vocals stem -> pseudo vocal-activity labels; accompaniment stem -> 'instrumental only' negatives matching real karaoke confusers; vocal stem energy -> frame-level vocal-energy teacher signal. Also fast enough (<10GB with --segment) to run at inference time on a whole VOD if you decide you want a vocal-energy feature online.

entries.name: BS-RoFormer (Band-Split RoPE Transformer, ByteDance)
entries.key_idea: Splits the complex spectrogram into 62 uneven subbands, then stacks hierarchical Transformers alternating along time and band axes with Rotary Position Embeddings; estimates complex multi-band masks. Won 1st place in the SDX23 Music Demixing track; smaller version reaches 9.80 dB avg SDR on MUSDB-HQ without extra data. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.02612 ; MIT reimplementation: https://github.com/lucidrains/BS-RoFormer
entries.params_and_speed: 72.2M params (L=6) per single stem, 93.4M for the L=12 competition variant; original training took 16x A100-80G for ~4 weeks (you would only fine-tune, not train from scratch). Inference: ~0.13s per chunk on RTX 4090 in PyTorch; TensorRT export was 2x SLOWER (Tile op bottleneck, https://github.com/lucidrains/BS-RoFormer/issues/37). Real-world: 3-min song = 1m49s on M3 Max via audio-separator; roughly 3-5x slower than HTDemucs but still multiple-x faster than real time on a 3090. Memory controllable via chunk size/batch; fits well under 24GB, can run under 10GB with small chunks.
entries.pretrained: No official ByteDance weights. Community: viperx checkpoint model_bs_roformer_ep_317_sdr_12.9755.ckpt (609MB file, vocal SDR 10.87 on Multisong benchmark), hosted in https://github.com/TRvlvr/model_repo releases (all_public_uvr_models tag, verified via GitHub API); loadable through audio-separator or ZFTurbo's framework. lucidrains code MIT.
entries.relevance: Highest-quality vocal/accompaniment split for offline hard-negative construction — its cleaner vocal stems make the 'other singer over the same accompaniment' remix negatives and speaker-embedding training data much less contaminated by bleed. Too heavy to justify in the online detector path, but fine for overnight pseudo-labeling of your training VODs on the 3090s.

entries.name: Mel-Band RoFormer (+ KimberleyJensen vocal model)
entries.key_idea: Variant of BS-RoFormer that replaces the heuristic non-overlapping band split with overlapped mel-scale subbands; outperforms BS-RoFormer on vocals and drums on MUSDB-HQ. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01809 . The community fine-tune by KimberleyJensen is the de-facto best open vocal extractor: vocal SDR 10.98 on ZFTurbo's Multisong benchmark, the top score among freely available vocal models (per https://github.com/ZFTurbo/Music-Source-Separation-Training/blob/main/docs/pretrained_models.md ).
entries.params_and_speed: Same RoFormer-class cost as BS-RoFormer (tens of millions of params per stem; checkpoint files 870MB-1.6GB in TRvlvr/model_repo, e.g. model_mel_band_roformer_ep_3005_sdr_11.4360.ckpt = 961MB). Expect RoFormer-tier speed: several x slower than HTDemucs, still >real-time on a 3090; runnable via audio-separator with chunked inference.
entries.pretrained: Kim vocal model: https://github.com/KimberleyJensen/Mel-Band-Roformer-Vocal-Model (no license file declared — verified via GitHub API; treat weights as research-use). Checkpoints also mirrored in TRvlvr/model_repo and auto-downloadable by python-audio-separator.
entries.relevance: Use this (or BS-RoFormer viperx) as the 'gold' separator for building the evaluation set and the hardest negatives; its near-zero instrumental bleed in the vocal stem means a simple energy/VAD threshold on the stem gives reliable frame-level singing pseudo-labels for person A's VODs.

entries.name: ZFTurbo Music-Source-Separation-Training
entries.key_idea: MIT-licensed training + inference framework that wraps lucidrains' RoFormer implementations plus MDX23C, HTDemucs, SCNet, VitLarge, etc., with a maintained pretrained-model zoo and a public 'Multisong' SDR leaderboard (BS-RoFormer viperx 10.87, Mel-RoFormer Kim 10.98, BS-PolarFormer 11.00, MDX23C 10.17 vocal SDR). https://github.com/ZFTurbo/Music-Source-Separation-Training and docs/pretrained_models.md. Supports tolerant checkpoint loading for fine-tuning on your own data.
entries.params_and_speed: Framework, not a model; per-model costs as listed in other entries. Fine-tuning RoFormer-class models fits on 24GB 3090s with small batch + short segments (the repo is explicitly designed for single/few-GPU experimenters).
entries.pretrained: MIT (verified via GitHub API), 1.4k stars; download links for all checkpoints in docs/pretrained_models.md.
entries.relevance: Single entry point for everything separation-related in AutoSlicer v2: run any separator for pseudo-labeling, and if you ever want a streamer-A-specialized vocal extractor (e.g. fine-tuned on her stems), this is the training harness that runs on your 3090s.

entries.name: MDX-Net / KUIELab-MDX-Net + UVR MDX-Net ONNX models (Kim_Vocal_2, UVR-MDX-NET-Voc_FT, Inst_HQ_4/5)
entries.key_idea: TFC-TDF U-Net v2 spectrogram models, one per stem, deliberately downsized + frequency-cut so each single-source model trains on an 11GB consumer GPU and infers within the MDX21 challenge time limit (2nd place Leaderboard A). Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.12203 ; code: https://github.com/kuielab/mdx-net (MIT, verified). The UVR community (Anjok07/aufr33/KimberleyJensen) trained much better vocal/instrumental MDX-Net ONNX checkpoints that are the workhorses of the UVR ecosystem.
entries.params_and_speed: ONNX model files are tiny: Kim_Vocal_2.onnx = 63MB, UVR-MDX-NET-Voc_FT.onnx = 63MB, Inst_HQ_4/5 = 56MB (verified from TRvlvr/model_repo release assets) — order ~15-30M params. Fastest neural option in audio-separator's own datapoint: 36s for a 3-min song on M3 Max CPU/Metal (vs 1m49s for BS-RoFormer); on a 3090 expect well over 10x real-time. VRAM footprint is small (a few GB).
entries.pretrained: Auto-downloaded by python-audio-separator (`--model_filename Kim_Vocal_2.onnx` etc.) from https://github.com/TRvlvr/model_repo/releases (all_public_uvr_models). UVR GUI repo is MIT; individual community model weights have no formal license — fine for internal tooling/pseudo-labeling.
entries.relevance: The 'cheap online separator' candidate: if you want a vocal-energy feature at inference time on a <10GB GPU, a 63MB MDX-Net vocal model is the best quality-per-FLOP choice. Vocal SDR is lower than RoFormer (~9-10 vs ~11) but plenty for an energy/activity feature.

entries.name: MDX23C (TFC-TDF-UNet v3)
entries.key_idea: KUIELab's SDX23-era successor to MDX-Net; checkpoint MDX23C-8KFFT-InstVoc_HQ is the audio-separator maintainer's quality runner-up after BS-RoFormer (vocal SDR 10.17 on the Multisong benchmark per ZFTurbo's table). Middle ground between MDX-Net speed and RoFormer quality. See https://github.com/ZFTurbo/Music-Source-Separation-Training and https://github.com/nomadkaraoke/python-audio-separator/discussions/133
entries.params_and_speed: Slower than MDX-Net v2 and even than BS-RoFormer in one datapoint: 2m37s for a 3-min song on M3 Max (audio-separator maintainer). On a 3090 still faster than real time, but it offers no clear win over Mel-RoFormer for offline use or MDX-Net for online use.
entries.pretrained: MDX23C-8KFFT-InstVoc_HQ.ckpt via audio-separator / TRvlvr model repo; framework MIT.
entries.relevance: Mostly useful as an ensemble diversifier if pseudo-label agreement between two different separator families (RoFormer + MDX23C) is used to filter noisy labels.

entries.name: python-audio-separator (nomadkaraoke) / UVR ecosystem
entries.key_idea: pip-installable CLI/library exposing all UVR model families (MDX-Net ONNX, VR-arch, Demucs, MDX23C, BS/Mel-RoFormer ckpts) behind one interface with automatic model download and an SDR-annotated `--list_models`. https://github.com/nomadkaraoke/python-audio-separator . Maintainer's measured speeds (same 3-min pop song, M3 Max): 2_HP-UVR 19s, MDX-NET-Inst_HQ_4 36s, BS-RoFormer ep317 1m49s, MDX23C 2m37s.
entries.params_and_speed: Wrapper; supports CUDA, batch processing. Whole-VOD batch separation of multi-hour audio is just a for-loop over chunks; on a 3090 a 6-hour VOD through MDX-Net or HTDemucs is tens of minutes.
entries.pretrained: Package MIT; models fetched from TRvlvr/model_repo and HuggingFace mirrors.
entries.relevance: The fastest path to a working pseudo-labeling pipeline tonight: one dependency gives you Demucs 2-stem, MDX-Net, and RoFormer checkpoints with consistent chunked GPU inference — no need to integrate three codebases.

entries.name: MVSEP-MDX23 (ZFTurbo SDX23 ensemble)
entries.key_idea: ZFTurbo's SDX23 Leaderboard-C 3rd-place system: ensemble of Demucs4 + multiple MDX(UVR) vocal models with multiband blending; has a `--single_onnx` cheap mode and community Colab forks with a 2-stem vocals/instrumental fast path. https://github.com/ZFTurbo/MVSEP-MDX23-music-separation-model and https://github.com/jarredou/MVSEP-MDX23-Colab_v2
entries.params_and_speed: Ensemble = sum of member costs (several x slower than any single model); GPU recommended, CPU 'will be slow' per README.
entries.pretrained: Repo public on GitHub; uses UVR/Kim Jensen weights.
entries.relevance: Demonstrates the ensemble-agreement trick for higher-precision vocal stems; for AutoSlicer it's overkill at inference but a reasonable 'maximum quality' offline option if single-model pseudo-labels look noisy. Mostly superseded by Mel-RoFormer Kim for pure vocal quality.

entries.name: Moises-Light
entries.key_idea: WASPAA 2025 paper from Moises/Music AI: DTTNet-style symmetric U-Net + efficient band-split + RoPE transformer bottleneck + SCNet-style encoder/decoder. ~5M params per stem reaches ~9.96 dB avg SDR on MUSDB-HQ (13x fewer params than BS-RoFormer, half of SCNet); a 2.2M-param small variant still beats DTTNet/TFC-TDF v3/HDemucs. https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.06785
entries.params_and_speed: 5M params (full), 2.2M (small) per single-stem model; trained on 2x H100 in <=5 days with 9s segments — i.e., the architecture class is reproducible on your 3090s. No public RTF figures, but at 5M params it is by far the lightest near-SOTA separator described to date.
entries.pretrained: None — no public weights found (company research; code not released as of June 2026).
entries.relevance: Proof-point that a ~5M-param band-split model gets within ~0.5-1 dB of RoFormer vocals quality. If you ever need an in-house, <10GB, faster-than-MDX online vocal-energy extractor, re-implementing/training this class (via ZFTurbo's framework, which already has band-split + RoPE blocks) is the efficient route — but it is a project, not a tonight task.

entries.name: SCNet (Sparse Compression Network)
entries.key_idea: Efficiency-oriented spectrogram separation net that compresses frequency bands sparsely (more capacity on vocal-critical low/mid bands); used as the encoder/decoder design basis in Moises-Light, with SCNet-S/L variants traded against RoFormers in recent benchmark tables. https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.13276 ; ZFTurbo's framework includes SCNet configs and checkpoints.
entries.params_and_speed: SCNet-S ~ a few M params, SCNet-L ~10M (roughly half of which Moises-Light halves again, per the Moises-Light comparison); substantially cheaper than 72M+ RoFormers at modestly lower SDR.
entries.pretrained: Community checkpoints available through ZFTurbo's Music-Source-Separation-Training model zoo (MIT framework).
entries.relevance: The currently-downloadable middle option between MDX-Net (fast, ~9-10 SDR) and RoFormer (slow, ~11 SDR) if you want a lighter separator you can also fine-tune yourself on 3090s.

entries.name: HPSS (harmonic/percussive source separation, median-filtering; librosa.effects.hpss)
entries.key_idea: Classical method: STFT -> median filtering along time (harmonic) vs frequency (percussive) -> soft/hard masks -> ISTFT. One function call in librosa (https://librosa.org/doc/latest/generated/librosa.effects.hpss.html, verified). Runs orders of magnitude faster than real time on CPU (it is just an STFT plus 2D median filters).
entries.params_and_speed: Zero learned parameters; CPU-only is fine; negligible memory. Effectively free as a feature extractor over multi-hour VODs.
entries.pretrained: N/A — librosa, ISC license.
entries.relevance: Critically: HPSS does NOT isolate vocals. Singing voice is predominantly harmonic, so it lands in the same component as guitars/synths/strings — exactly the content of your confusers (a)-(c). HPSS can only serve as an auxiliary input feature (e.g., percussive-residual ratio) for the classifier, not as a vocal-presence signal. Same caveat for raw vocal-band (≈200Hz-4kHz) energy ratios: they cannot distinguish a lead-guitar melody or another singer's recording from streamer A singing, so they fail confusers (b)/(d). Cheap features may still help the classifier as extra channels, but don't build the decision on them.

recommendations:
1) Use separation OFFLINE as a data factory, not as a core online dependency. Tonight, install python-audio-separator (one pip dependency, MIT) and run two separators over your labeled VOD segments and song library: (a) HTDemucs `--two-stems=vocals` (fast pass, ~42M params, fits 3-7GB, >10x RT on a 3090) for bulk processing, and (b) Mel-RoFormer Kim (vocal SDR ~10.98) or BS-RoFormer viperx ep317 for a high-quality pass on the training/eval sets. A 6-hour VOD through HTDemucs on a 3090 is tens of minutes; RoFormer is a few hours — both fine overnight across several 3090s.

2) Build all four confuser classes synthetically from the stems: (a) accompaniment-only stems = 'pure instrumental, no singing' negatives that share the exact backing-track distribution of real karaoke segments; (b) random unmatched accompaniments; (c) truncated/partial accompaniments; (d) the killer hard negative — take the accompaniment stem from streamer A's performance and mix in the vocal stem of the ORIGINAL artist (separated from the official recording) at matched loudness. This directly teaches the model that 'singing present' is not enough — it must be person A. Also use A's clean vocal stems to train/calibrate the speaker-ID head (a speaker/singer embedding on the separated vocal stem is far more discriminative than on the mixture).

3) Pseudo-labeling: frame-level vocal activity = smoothed energy (or a tiny VAD) on the RoFormer vocal stem; where HTDemucs and RoFormer stems disagree strongly, drop or down-weight the frames (cheap ensemble-agreement filtering, in the spirit of MVSEP-MDX23).

4) Online vocal-energy feature — only if ablations show you need it. Your 1-2x RT budget actually permits it: even PyTorch HTDemucs 2-stem at >10x RT consumes <10% of the budget and <7GB VRAM (3GB with --segment 8), and a 63MB MDX-Net ONNX vocal model (Kim_Vocal_2 / Voc_FT) is cheaper still. But the cleaner design is distillation: compute the vocal-stem energy envelope offline with RoFormer, then train your detector with an auxiliary regression head that predicts it from the mixture. You get the separator's signal at zero inference cost.

5) Skip HPSS and vocal-band energy ratios as decision features — vocals are harmonic, so HPSS cannot separate singing from melodic instruments, and band-energy ratios fail confusers (b) and (d) by construction. At most, feed them as extra input channels.

6) Avoid TensorRT for RoFormer (measured 2x slower than PyTorch due to a Tile-op bottleneck on RTX 4090); plain PyTorch with fp16/bf16 autocast and large chunk batches is the right deployment for RoFormer-class models. TensorRT DOES pay off for HTDemucs (~5s/song on a 3090) if you later need maximum offline throughput.

7) Licensing note for anything user-facing: demucs, UVR GUI, kuielab/mdx-net, lucidrains/BS-RoFormer, and ZFTurbo's framework are all MIT (verified), but the popular community CHECKPOINTS (viperx, KimberleyJensen, UVR ONNX models) carry no formal license — fine for internal pseudo-labeling/augmentation, but flag it if a separator ships inside the product.

summary: Singing voice detection (SVD) is a mature MIR task: frame-level binary vocal/non-vocal classification, benchmarked almost entirely on Jamendo Corpus (93 CC songs, 61/16/16 split) and RWC Popular Music. The 2015-era strong baselines (Schlüter & Grill CNN on log-mel with augmentation; Lehner LSTM on 111 hand-crafted features; Leglaive HPSS+BLSTM) sit at 89-92% frame accuracy on Jamendo, and a decade of follow-up work (CRNN/LRCN, GRU-RNN, ensembles) has only pushed this to ~92-94%, suggesting label noise/annotation ambiguity caps the benchmark. The dominant 2020-2024 trend is to run music source separation (Demucs family) first and detect voice on the separated vocal stem — this both raises accuracy (0.924 with an LRCN on Demucs vocals, MLSP 2023) and, crucially for AutoSlicer, trivially rejects pure-instrumental confusers. The second trend is frozen self-supervised frontends (MERT, wav2vec2.0, WavLM) probed with light heads, which match or beat supervised SOTA on singing tasks with little labeled data.
