Pure SVD is no longer a hot topic at ISMIR/ICASSP 2023-2026; activity moved to separation-assisted pipelines (SingFake's Demucs+pyannote VAD), singing deepfake detection, and audio-visual VAD in live musical streams (ByteDance ICASSP 2021 — the closest published analog to the streamer-VOD setting, treating speech/singing/other as a 3-class frame task). Typical feature configs: 22.05kHz audio, 80 log-mel bands, n_fft 1024, hop ~315 samples (~70 fps) for the classic CNN; ~100 ms frame granularity is the standard output resolution. The speech-vs-singing boundary is handled poorly in the classic literature (rap/humming are acknowledged failure modes; pitch-stability/pitch-histogram features are the classic discriminator); none of the standard SVD models distinguish WHICH singer is singing, so the person-A requirement and the 'same song by another singer' confuser require a singer-identity component on top of SVD.

entries.name: Schlüter & Grill — Exploring Data Augmentation for Improved Singing Voice Detection with Neural Networks (ISMIR)
entries.key_idea: 3-conv-layer CNN on log-magnitude mel spectrograms classifying the center frame of a 115-frame (~1.6 s) excerpt. Input: audio resampled to 22.05 kHz, frame length 1024, hop 315 samples (~70 fps), 80 mel bands, per-band zero-mean/unit-variance normalization. Key contribution is spectrogram-domain augmentation (pitch shifting, time stretching, random frequency filtering) which cuts error to ~9% (≈91-92% frame accuracy, ~0.90 recall) on Jamendo — still cited as the canonical strong CNN baseline. Paper: https://archives.ismir.net/ismir2015/paper/000264.pdf ; follow-up 'Zero-Mean Convolutions for Level-Invariant Singing Voice Detection' (ISMIR 2018, https://www.ofai.at/~jan.schlueter/pubs/2018_ismir.pdf) fixes its sensitivity to absolute loudness.
entries.params_and_speed: Small CNN (~1M-scale params); runs orders of magnitude faster than real time on any modern GPU; trivially fits <10GB.
entries.pretrained: Official reimplementation https://github.com/f0k/ismir2015 (MIT license, verified via GitHub API); trainable from scratch on Jamendo in hours.
entries.relevance: Defines the proven recipe (log-mel + short-context CNN + heavy augmentation) and the realistic accuracy ceiling (~92%) for generic SVD. Its 1.6 s receptive field shows long context is NOT required for the vocal/non-vocal decision itself — your old system's minutes-of-context need likely came from the singer-identity/segmentation part, not SVD.

entries.name: Lehner et al. — LSTM-RNN singing voice detection ('Lehner-3')
entries.key_idea: BiLSTM-free unidirectional LSTM-RNN over 111 hand-crafted frame features (30 MFCCs + first-order deltas + spectral features like fluctogram/spectral contraction designed to reject pitch-continuous instruments that cause false positives). Reported 0.848-0.894 accuracy, ~0.902 F1 on Jamendo; was SOTA-competitive with much lower latency than CNN ensembles. Summarized with exact numbers in the SVD survey https://www.mdpi.com/1099-4300/24/1/114 and in Lee et al. 2018 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.01180).
entries.params_and_speed: Tiny (<1M params); designed for low-latency/real-time operation on CPU.
entries.pretrained: No maintained official weights; reproduced in https://github.com/kyungyunlee/ismir2018-revisiting-svd.
entries.relevance: Shows hand-engineered anti-confuser features (fluctogram etc.) targeting exactly your confuser class (b)/(c): solo instruments mimicking voice. Mostly historical now — separation front-ends solve this more robustly — but a useful sanity baseline.

entries.name: Lee, Choi & Nam — Revisiting Singing Voice Detection: A Quantitative Review and the Future Outlook (ISMIR 2018)
entries.key_idea: Error analysis of the three classic systems (Lehner LSTM, Schlüter CNN, Leglaive HPSS+BLSTM, the latter at 91.5% acc / 0.910 F1 on Jamendo) using curated probe sets: vibrato-only synthetic tones, varying vocal/accompaniment SNR, and MedleyDB-derived labels. Reveals systematic failure modes not visible in Jamendo scores — electric guitar and pitch-fluctuating instruments trigger false positives, performance degrades sharply at low vocal SNR, and annotation ambiguity (humming, background vocals) bounds measurable accuracy. arXiv: https://arxiv.org/abs/1806.01180 , official PDF: https://ismir2018.ismir.net/doc/pdfs/38_Paper.pdf
entries.params_and_speed: n/a (analysis paper); the three reproduced models are all small (<10M).
entries.pretrained: Code + 3 reproduced models: https://github.com/kyungyunlee/ismir2018-revisiting-svd (also generates vibrato/SNR test sets).
entries.relevance: The definitive map of WHERE SVD models fail — directly enumerates your confusers (a)-(c). Its SNR-sweep methodology is a ready-made evaluation protocol for testing AutoSlicer against karaoke backing tracks at different vocal levels.

entries.name: LRCN + vocal separation + temporal smoothing (Electronics 2020; electronic-music extension Appl. Sci. 2022 + Electrobyte dataset)
entries.key_idea: Two-stage pipeline: singing-voice separation first coarsely removes accompaniment, then a Long-term Recurrent Convolutional Network (CNN feature extractor + LSTM) classifies frames, followed by temporal smoothing (median/HMM-style) of the frame posterior. Reached state-of-the-art on Jamendo and RWC Popular (survey notes RWC scores are always higher than Jamendo for every algorithm, and GRU variants beat LSTM on both accuracy and efficiency). 2022 extension built 'Electrobyte' (90 electronic songs, 60/15/15) because EDM breaks vocal/instrumental assumptions. Papers: https://www.mdpi.com/2079-9292/9/9/1458 , https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/12/15/7405 , related arXiv https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.04040
entries.params_and_speed: LRCN itself small (few M params); dominated by the separation front-end cost.
entries.pretrained: No widely-used released weights; architecture easy to reproduce.
entries.relevance: Validates the exact architecture family you'd retrain tonight (conv tower + recurrent head + smoothing) and confirms separation-as-preprocessing is the accuracy lever. Temporal smoothing of frame posteriors is the standard way to get clean song-boundary segments for clipping.

entries.name: Singing Voice Detection: A Survey (Entropy, 2022)
entries.key_idea: Comprehensive survey of SVD methods through 2021: features (MFCC weakness against pitch-continuous instruments, dominance of log-mel), architectures (CNN, LSTM/GRU-RNN, LRCN, ensembles), and benchmark tables. Key reference numbers on Jamendo: Schlüter CNN 0.923 acc, Leglaive BLSTM 0.915, CNN-spectrogram 91.8% rising to 94.2% with a 5-model voting ensemble; LRCN listed as SOTA on both Jamendo and RWC. Jamendo = 93 copyright-free songs (61 train / 16 val / 16 test) with frame-level vocal annotations; RWC Popular = 100 songs. https://www.mdpi.com/1099-4300/24/1/114 (also PMC: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8775013/)
entries.params_and_speed: n/a (survey).
entries.pretrained: n/a.
entries.relevance: One-stop source for typical accuracies/F1 (≈90-94% frame accuracy is the achievable band on public data — consistent with your >=80% floor and >90% stretch goal) and for dataset choices if you want public pretraining data before fine-tuning on streamer-A VODs.

entries.name: Bonzi et al. — Exploiting Music Source Separation for Singing Voice Detection (IEEE MLSP 2023, Sapienza)
entries.key_idea: Trains LRCN and Transformer SVD models on (a) raw mixtures vs (b) Demucs-separated vocal stems on Jamendo; LRCN on separated vocals achieves 0.924 accuracy, beating mixture-trained models, demonstrating that a modern separator front-end transfers most of the discrimination burden away from the classifier. PDF: https://gladia.di.uniroma1.it/publication/bonzi-2023-voice/bonzi-2023-voice.pdf
entries.params_and_speed: Classifier is small; Demucs (HTDemucs) dominates compute but runs several times faster than real time on a 24GB GPU and fits well under 10GB at inference with segment-wise processing.
entries.pretrained: Demucs: https://github.com/facebookresearch/demucs — MIT license, 10.2k stars, now archived (verified via GitHub API); maintained forks exist. Paper code not prominently released.
entries.relevance: This is the design pattern AutoSlicer v2 should copy: separate vocals → detect voice on the stem. Confusers (a) pure instrumental, (b) non-matching instrumental, (c) partial instrumental all become near-trivial because the vocal stem is essentially silent; you then only need a voice detector + singer verifier on the stem.

entries.name: SingFake / SVDD pipeline — Demucs + pyannote VAD segmentation (ICASSP 2024) and SingGraph (Interspeech 2024)
entries.key_idea: SingFake (https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.07525) curates 58h of real/deepfake song clips and its preprocessing pipeline is directly reusable: Demucs separates vocals, then pyannote's VAD produces timecodes of sung segments — a production-grade 'find singing regions in full songs' recipe. SingGraph (https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.03111) shows MERT (pitch/rhythm) + wav2vec2.0 (linguistic) embeddings are complementary for discriminating singers/voices over backing music, with relative EER gains of 13-37%. The SVDD 2024 challenge institutionalized the task.
entries.params_and_speed: pyannote segmentation model is ~1.5M params, faster than real time on CPU; Demucs as above; SingGraph-class models ~100-400M (MERT+wav2vec2 backbones).
entries.pretrained: Demucs (MIT, GitHub); pyannote models on HuggingFace (pyannote/voice-activity-detection, MIT code, gated weights requiring HF terms acceptance); SingGraph code on GitHub.
entries.relevance: Two direct payoffs: (1) the Demucs→pyannote-VAD pipeline is an off-the-shelf zero-training baseline for finding sung segments in VODs; (2) the deepfake-detection literature proves voice-identity over background music is learnable — the same machinery (singer embeddings on separated vocals) handles your confuser (d), a recording of the same song by another singer.

entries.name: Yamamoto — Toward Leveraging Pre-Trained Self-Supervised Frontends for Singing Voice Understanding (arXiv 2306.12714) + MERT (arXiv 2306.00107, ICLR 2024)
entries.key_idea: Benchmarks frozen SSL frontends (wav2vec2.0, WavLM, MERT, Music2Vec — each raw-waveform CNN + 12 Transformer layers, 768-d frames) on singer identification, singing transcription, and singing technique classification; with light heads and layer-weighted pooling they match or beat supervised SOTA with limited labels. MERT itself uses RVQ-VAE acoustic + CQT musical teachers for masked prediction, scaling 95M→330M, and is the strongest music-domain encoder for pitch-centric tasks; probing studies note WavLM/HuBERT underperform on singing because their pretraining contains no singing voice (https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.24793).
entries.params_and_speed: MERT-v1-95M: 95M params (~0.4GB fp16), 75 Hz output frame rate at 24 kHz input; wav2vec2-base 95M at 50 Hz / 16 kHz. Frozen-encoder inference is many times faster than real time on a 3090 and fits <10GB easily.
entries.pretrained: HuggingFace: m-a-p/MERT-v1-95M and m-a-p/MERT-v1-330M (license CC-BY-NC-4.0 as listed on the model cards — non-commercial; verify against your use); facebook/wav2vec2-base (Apache-2.0); microsoft/wavlm-base-plus (MIT).
entries.relevance: Best path to >>90% with one night of 3090 training: freeze MERT-95M (or wav2vec2 on separated vocals), train a small temporal head for joint {speech / A-singing / other-singing / no-voice} classification on a few hours of labeled VOD. Combining a music encoder (MERT) and a speech encoder (wav2vec2) mirrors SingGraph and covers both the SVD and singer-ID aspects.

entries.name: Hou et al. — Transfer Learning for Improving Singing-Voice Detection in Polyphonic Instrumental Music (Interspeech 2020, arXiv 2008.04658)
entries.key_idea: Synthesizes training data by mixing clean speech (with VAD endpoints) over instrumental music, trains a vocal detector on the artificial mixtures, then transfer-learns onto a small genuinely-sung polyphonic set to bridge the speaking-vs-singing articulation/phonation mismatch; F-score improves 89.5% → 93.2%. https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.04658
entries.params_and_speed: Small CNN/RNN detector; negligible inference cost.
entries.pretrained: none (recipe paper).
entries.relevance: A cheap data-amplification recipe for tonight's training: you have hours of streamer-A speech and the actual karaoke backing tracks from VODs — synthetically mixing A's voice (speech and any isolated singing) over those exact instrumentals creates large, perfectly-labeled, in-domain training data, including hard negatives (instrumental-only mixes) matching confusers (a)-(c).

entries.name: Rule-embedded audio-visual VAD in live musical video streams (ByteDance, ICASSP 2021, arXiv 2010.14168)
entries.key_idea: The closest published analog to the streamer-VOD problem: detect when the ANCHOR of a live musical stream speaks or sings, in a cocktail-party setting where background music, other voices, or a reference recording may play simultaneously. Uses an audio branch (frame-level speech/singing/others classification) fused with a visual branch (mouth/face activity) via an embedded rule that uses visual representations as a mask to filter non-target sound; A-V model far outperforms audio-only. Introduces a frame-labeled live musical video corpus. https://arxiv.org/abs/2010.14168
entries.params_and_speed: CRNN-scale audio branch + light visual CNN; real-time capable (designed for live streams).
entries.pretrained: Dataset/code release limited; treat as a design reference.
entries.relevance: Direct evidence for your hardest confuser (d): audio-only models cannot reliably tell 'A is singing' from 'a recording of someone singing is playing', but the video track (A's mouth moving in sync) resolves it. Since VODs include video, an optional lightweight visual mouth-activity check on detected segments is a proven disambiguation lever.

entries.name: PANNs CNN14 / panns_inference (audio tagging with AudioSet 'Singing' classes)
entries.key_idea: CNNs pretrained on AudioSet (5000 h, 527 classes incl. 'Singing', 'Male singing', 'Female singing', 'Choir', 'Rapping', 'Music'); Wavegram-Logmel-CNN reaches mAP 0.439. DecisionLevelMax/Att variants give frame-wise sound event detection (onset/duration), usable as a weak frame-level singing labeler out of the box. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.10211 ; repo: https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn
entries.params_and_speed: CNN14 ≈ 80M params; 32 kHz input, 64-mel log spectrogram; inference well over 10x real time on GPU, runs in <4GB; pip install panns_inference.
entries.pretrained: Checkpoints on Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/record/3987831, e.g. Cnn14_mAP=0.431.pth); repo MIT-licensed (verified via GitHub API). Newer alternatives: AST/BEATs/EfficientAT give higher mAP at similar cost.
entries.relevance: Zero-training bootstrap tool: run CNN14 (DecisionLevel variant) over whole VODs tonight to weak-label candidate singing regions, then hand-correct only those regions to build the fine-tuning set — converting the labeling bottleneck into a review task. Also a useful ensemble veto ('Music' high + 'Singing' low ⇒ instrumental confuser).

entries.name: Speech-vs-singing discrimination via pitch-derived parameters (Appl. Sci. 2019) + UT-Sing line of work
entries.key_idea: The speech/singing boundary literature consistently finds the discriminator is prosodic/pitch dynamics, not timbre: singing has piecewise-stable pitch locked to a scale (low variance in the DFT of the pitch histogram), longer voiced segments and stable syllable durations, while speech has continuous declination. GMM/short+long-term-feature systems and MAP-adapted models on the UT-Sing database formalize this. Rap and humming are the acknowledged gray zone — rap pitch behaves like speech (AudioSet even separates a 'Rapping' class), and Jamendo-style annotations treat any vocalization (incl. humming) as 'vocal', so model behavior on rap is dataset-convention-dependent. https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/9/15/3140 , https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7438159
entries.params_and_speed: Feature-level methods, negligible cost (pitch tracker + statistics).
entries.pretrained: none.
entries.relevance: For AutoSlicer the speech/singing boundary is THE core decision (a streamer talks for hours and sings occasionally). Pitch-stability statistics over a few-second window (e.g., from CREPE/pYIN on the separated vocal stem) are a cheap, interpretable feature to add to the classifier head, and you must define a policy for rap/humming segments up front because the literature gives no consistent convention.

recommendations:
1) Adopt the separation-first architecture, not a bigger end-to-end net. The clear 2020-2024 consensus (LRCN+separation papers, MLSP 2023, SingFake) is: HTDemucs (MIT, GPU, several x real time, <10GB) -> vocal stem -> frame classifier. This structurally eliminates confusers (a)-(c): a karaoke backing track with no singing yields a near-silent vocal stem, so even a simple energy/VAD gate rejects it, whereas your 2021 end-to-end SincConv/ResNeXt model had to learn that invariance from data. Budget: separation is the dominant inference cost; cache stems.

2) Make the classifier a 4-class frame model on the stem, not binary SVD: {no-voice, speech, target-A-singing, other-voice-singing}. Generic SVD plateaus at 92-94% frame accuracy on Jamendo and knows nothing about WHO sings; your task is really SVD x singer verification. Recommended head: frozen MERT-v1-95M (75 Hz frames; note CC-BY-NC license — use wav2vec2-base/WavLM, Apache/MIT, if that matters) on the vocal stem, plus a singer-embedding branch (ECAPA-TDNN or the SingGraph-style wav2vec2 branch) trained with A-vs-others contrastive labels. A 2-4 layer Transformer/BiGRU head over ~10-30 s context trains in well under a night on one 3090; the whole stack infers far faster than real time in <10GB.

3) Solve confuser (d) — same song, different singer — with singer identity, and optionally video. Audio-only evidence: SingFake/SingGraph show voice identity over backing music is learnable (EER gains 13-37% with MERT+wav2vec2). Stronger evidence: the ByteDance live-stream AV-VAD paper (arXiv:2010.14168) shows fusing a cheap visual mouth-activity check resolves exactly the 'reference recording is playing' ambiguity; since you have VOD video, run a lightweight face/mouth-motion correlation only on detected singing segments (negligible cost, big precision win).

4) Manufacture training data tonight instead of hand-labeling everything. (i) Run PANNs CNN14 DecisionLevel (pip install panns_inference, MIT) over all VODs to weak-label candidate singing regions, then human-verify only those — this converts labeling into review. (ii) Use the Interspeech-2020 transfer recipe (arXiv:2008.04658): mix streamer-A clean speech/singing snippets over the actual backing tracks at varied SNRs to generate perfectly-labeled in-domain positives AND instrumental-only hard negatives; their F-score went 89.5->93.2 with this. (iii) Apply Schlüter-style augmentation (pitch shift, time stretch, frequency filtering) — it is the single most reliable accuracy lever in SVD history.

5) Features/frame rate: stay with log-mel (or SSL frames); the classic config is 22.05 kHz, 80 mels, hop ~315 (~70 fps) with ~1.6 s receptive field for the local decision; output decisions at 50-100 ms resolution, then median-filter/HMM-smooth posteriors and merge segments with hysteresis (high threshold to open a segment, low to close, min-duration ~10-20 s, gap-bridging ~5 s) to produce clean per-song cuts. Long context (minutes) is unnecessary for detection — your old system needed it only because it lacked separation and identity signals.

6) Evaluation: build a probe-set protocol following Lee et al. ISMIR 2018 — held-out VODs plus targeted sets for each confuser: instrumental-only karaoke segments, partial instrumentals, original-artist recordings of songs A also sings, and rap/humming edge cases (decide and document the rap/humming policy now; the literature has no consistent convention). Report segment-level precision/recall at the clip level (what users see), not just frame accuracy. Given Jamendo SOTA is ~92-94% in a much harder open-domain setting, >90% segment-level accuracy on a single known singer with separation + identity + (optional) video is realistic.

summary: Survey of modern audio tagging / SED backbones (2020-2025) for frame-level singing detection in long VODs. Key findings: (1) The field has converged on AudioSet-pretrained backbones + a light frame-level head; (2) For your constraints (3090 training tonight, <10GB inference, 1-2x RT minimum), the efficient distilled models are the sweet spot: EfficientAT MobileNetV3 (mn10, 4.88M params, 0.47 mAP, ~61x faster than PaSST on GPU), its frame-wise successors fmn/frame_mn10 (3.83M, 40ms frame resolution, PSDS1 ~45 on AudioSet-Strong, near transformer SOTA), and CED-Tiny/Mini (5.5M/9.6M, 0.48-0.49 mAP); (3) For maximum accuracy, frame-level-pretrained transformers from fschmid56/PretrainedSED (BEATs/ATST-Frame/fPaSST/M2D pre-trained on AudioSet-Strong with ensemble KD, MIT license) are directly fine-tunable and are exactly what DCASE Task 4 winners build on; (4) The proven low-data recipe is frozen BEATs embeddings + CRNN + mean-teacher (DCASE 2023/2024 baseline and winner pattern), designed for regimes with only a few hours of strongly-labeled audio; (5) All inference-speed constraints are non-binding — even 90M-param transformers run 100x+ real-time on a 10GB GPU at 10s-clip granularity; the binding constraints are fine-tune data efficiency and frame resolution (BEATs 160ms vs ATST-Frame/fmn 40ms). Note: distinguishing streamer A from a played-back recording of another singer is a singer-identity problem that none of these tagging backbones solve alone — it needs a speaker/singer-embedding branch or channel/acoustic-mismatch cues on top.

entries.name: PANNs (CNN14 / CNN10 / CNN6, MobileNetV1/V2, Wavegram-Logmel-CNN)
entries.key_idea: Large-scale supervised CNN pretraining on AudioSet (1.9M clips, 527 classes) for transfer to downstream audio tasks. CNN14 (mAP 0.431) is the workhorse; Wavegram-Logmel-CNN combines learned waveform features with log-mel (mAP 0.439, ~81M params, 53.5 G multi-adds). The repo also ships DecisionLevel (framewise) SED variants. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.10211, code: https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn (MIT, verified via GitHub API).
entries.params_and_speed: CNN14 ~81M; CNN10/CNN6 smaller; CNN14 ~42 G multi-adds per 10s clip at 32kHz/64-mel. Runs well within 10GB; many x real-time but slower per-mAP than EfficientAT.
entries.pretrained: Checkpoints on Zenodo linked from https://github.com/qiuqiangkong/audioset_tagging_cnn; repo license MIT.
entries.relevance: Your old SincConv/ResNeXt system's modern CNN ancestor; the Cnn14_DecisionLevelMax/Att variants natively output per-frame probabilities, which is the exact output shape you need. Superseded in accuracy-per-FLOP by EfficientAT/CED but still a safe, simple, MIT-licensed baseline that fine-tunes on a 3090 in hours.

entries.name: AST (Audio Spectrogram Transformer)
entries.key_idea: First convolution-free ViT applied to 128-mel spectrograms (16x16 patches, 10ms hop), initialized from ImageNet ViT/DeiT, fine-tuned on AudioSet to 0.485 mAP. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.01778 era work by Gong et al.; code: https://github.com/YuanGongND/ast (BSD-3-Clause). Clip-level by design (CLS token); per-frame output requires reading patch tokens, not natively supported.
entries.params_and_speed: ~86-87M (ViT-Base). Quadratic attention over long token sequences makes training memory-heavy; HTS-AT paper reports AST needs ~3x params and ~7x training time vs HTS-AT.
entries.pretrained: HuggingFace MIT/ast-finetuned-audioset-10-10-0.4593 (https://huggingface.co/MIT/ast-finetuned-audioset-10-10-0.4593), in transformers library; original repo BSD-3-Clause.
entries.relevance: Historically load-bearing but not recommended for you: expensive to fine-tune on 3090s, clip-level outputs, and every successor below beats it on accuracy/cost. Included as the reference architecture all the frame-level transformers (ATST-Frame, fPaSST) are derived from.

entries.name: PaSST (Patchout faSt Spectrogram Transformer)
entries.key_idea: AST-style transformer trained with 'patchout' — dropping random or structured (whole time-frame / frequency-bin) patches during training — giving up to 8x faster training and <10% of GPU memory while improving mAP (0.476 single, 0.486 with KD on AudioSet). Trains to SOTA in <2 days on one consumer GPU. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.05069 (Interspeech 2022); code: https://github.com/kkoutini/PaSST (Apache-2.0, verified).
entries.params_and_speed: ~87M (ViT-B, patch16); PaSST-L light variant depth=7. A100 throughput benchmark: 78 clips/sec (vs 4767 for mn10). Still 100x+ real-time for 10s clips.
entries.pretrained: Checkpoints auto-download via repo or passt_hear21 (https://github.com/kkoutini/passt_hear21); Apache-2.0.
entries.relevance: Clip-level natively, but the frame-level variant fPaSST (with AudioSet-Strong pretraining) is published in fschmid56/PretrainedSED. PaSST is also the teacher that EfficientAT models were distilled from. Patchout makes 3090 fine-tuning feasible overnight if you want a big-model teacher for your own distillation.

entries.name: HTS-AT (Hierarchical Token-Semantic Audio Transformer)
entries.key_idea: Swin-style hierarchical windowed-attention audio transformer (4 stages, downsampling token count) plus a 'token-semantic' CNN head that maps final tokens to per-class time-frequency featuremaps — giving event localization (frame-level output) trained only from weakly-labeled AudioSet. 35% of AST's params, 15% of its training time; beats PANNs on DESED localization (avg F1 50.7%). Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.00874 (ICASSP 2022); code: https://github.com/RetroCirce/HTS-Audio-Transformer (MIT, verified).
entries.params_and_speed: ~30-31M params; much lower memory than AST due to windowed attention; comfortably trains on a 24GB 3090.
entries.pretrained: Checkpoints for AudioSet, ESC-50, SpeechCommands, DESED in the GitHub repo (MIT). Also the audio encoder inside LAION-CLAP.
entries.relevance: Directly relevant pattern for you: weak labels in (whole-song segments labeled 'A singing'), per-frame localization out via the token-semantic head. Mid-size compromise between tiny CNNs and 90M transformers. Caveat: needs warm-up scheduling and is fussier to fine-tune than CNNs.

entries.name: BEATs (Microsoft)
entries.key_idea: Iterative self-supervised pretraining where an acoustic tokenizer and a masked-prediction audio transformer bootstrap each other; SOTA audio-only AudioSet mAP 50.6 (AS-2M). Outputs a sequence of patch-token embeddings (effective temporal resolution ~160ms), which is why it became the de-facto frozen feature extractor for DCASE Task 4 SED. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.09058; code+checkpoints: https://github.com/microsoft/unilm/tree/master/beats (repo MIT, verified via GitHub API; checkpoints on OneDrive).
entries.params_and_speed: ~90M (ViT-B-like: 12 layers, 768 dim). Fits inference in <10GB easily; ~100x+ real-time on modern GPUs for 10s windows. Full fine-tune on one 3090 is tight but feasible; common practice is to freeze it.
entries.pretrained: OneDrive links in the unilm README (BEATs_iter3+ AS2M recommended); improved frame-level checkpoint BEATs_strong_1.pt in https://github.com/fschmid56/PretrainedSED (MIT).
entries.relevance: The highest-accuracy frozen-embedding option and the backbone of every recent DCASE Task 4 winner/baseline. For a few hours of labeled singing data, frozen BEATs + small CRNN head is the most proven low-data recipe. Weakness for you: 160ms frame resolution (coarser than ATST-Frame's 40ms) — fine for song-boundary detection at ~1s tolerance.

entries.name: Audio-MAE (Masked Autoencoders that Listen, Meta)
entries.key_idea: MAE-style masked-spectrogram-reconstruction pretraining: encode only the ~20% unmasked patches, decode with local windowed attention to reconstruct; fine-tuned AudioSet mAP ~47.3. NeurIPS 2022, paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.06405; code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/AudioMAE (CC-BY 4.0 per repo README).
entries.params_and_speed: ViT-B encoder (~86M). Similar inference cost to AST/BEATs.
entries.pretrained: Checkpoints in the facebookresearch repo (CC-BY 4.0); community timm conversion at https://huggingface.co/gaunernst/vit_base_patch16_1024_128.audiomae_as2m. Requires old timm==0.3.2 patching — codebase is dated.
entries.relevance: Included for completeness as the MAE lineage that EAT improves on. No native frame-level head, dated tooling, and BEATs/EAT dominate it on accuracy — not recommended as your pick.

entries.name: EAT (Efficient Audio Transformer)
entries.key_idea: Bootstrapped SSL (data2vec-2.0-style teacher-student) on spectrograms with a novel Utterance-Frame Objective (clip-level + masked frame-level regression) and large inverse-block masking; matches/beats BEATs on AS-2M/AS-20K/ESC-50 with ~15x less pretraining compute than BEATs (10 epochs on AS-2M). IJCAI 2024, paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.03497; code: https://github.com/cwx-worst-one/EAT (MIT, verified).
entries.params_and_speed: EAT-base ~ViT-B scale (~88M), EAT-large bigger. Inference cost comparable to BEATs; >1M total HF downloads.
entries.pretrained: HuggingFace-integrated checkpoints (worstchan/EAT-base_epoch30_* etc.) linked from the GitHub repo; MIT license, no fairseq needed for inference.
entries.relevance: A modern, MIT-licensed, HF-friendly BEATs alternative whose UFO objective explicitly trains frame-level targets, so its frame embeddings transfer well to SED-style heads. Good candidate if you want a big frozen encoder with cleaner tooling than microsoft/unilm.

entries.name: CED (Consistent Ensemble Distillation) tiny/mini/small/base
entries.key_idea: Distills ViT students from large teacher ensembles with augmentation-consistent stored logits (label-free, scalable). Works on 16kHz / 64-mel input, deliberately cheap: CED-Tiny 5.5M params hits AS-2M mAP 48.1, CED-Mini 9.6M hits 49.0, CED-Small 22M / 49.6, CED-Base 86M / 50.0. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.11957; code: https://github.com/RicherMans/CED.
entries.params_and_speed: 5.5M / 9.6M / 22M / 86M. CED-Tiny is claimed as fast as MobileNetV3 on a single x86 CPU — GPU inference for a whole VOD is trivially faster than real-time and far under 10GB.
entries.pretrained: HuggingFace mispeech/ced-tiny|mini|small|base (model cards tagged Apache-2.0, https://huggingface.co/mispeech/ced-base); but the GitHub training code at RicherMans/CED is GPL-3.0 (verified by fetching the LICENSE file) — use the HF weights + your own fine-tune code if GPL is a concern.
entries.relevance: Best accuracy-per-parameter among transformers; CED-Mini at 9.6M nearly matches 80M-class models. ViT patch tokens give ~160ms columns (16-frame patches at 10ms hop) you can head with a per-frame classifier. Strong candidate for your 'small but accurate' track; mind the GPL/Apache license split.

entries.name: EfficientAT (mn04-mn40, dymn; MobileNetV3 distilled from PaSST ensemble)
entries.key_idea: Offline knowledge distillation from a 9-model PaSST ensemble (mAP .495) into ImageNet-initialized MobileNetV3 CNNs; mn10 matches single-PaSST-class accuracy at ~6% of the size. Verified README table: mn04 0.98M/0.11 GMACs/43.2 mAP; mn10 4.88M/0.54 GMACs/47.1; mn40 68.4M/8.0 GMACs/48.4; dynamic dymn10 10.6M/47.7, dymn20 40M/49.1. ICASSP 2023 paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.04772; code: https://github.com/fschmid56/EfficientAT (MIT, verified).
entries.params_and_speed: mn10: 4.88M params, ~0.5 GMACs per 10s clip (32kHz, 128 mel, 10ms hop). Measured 4767 clips/sec on A100 bs=200 vs 78 for PaSST (61x) — i.e., tens of thousands of x real-time; a 10GB GPU chews a 6-hour VOD in seconds-to-minutes.
entries.pretrained: Auto-downloaded via pretrained_name in the repo (MIT). Variants with fully-convolutional heads ('fc') and reduced strides exist for finer time resolution.
entries.relevance: The pragmatic backbone for your speed budget. CNN feature maps retain the time axis, so a frame-level head is natural — and the authors already did that for you (see PretrainedSED/EfficientSED frame-wise MobileNets). Fine-tunes in well under a night on one 3090 with a few hours of labels.

entries.name: PretrainedSED — 'Effective Pre-Training of Audio Transformers for Sound Event Detection' (Schmid et al., ICASSP 2025)
entries.key_idea: Adds a third pretraining stage — AudioSet-Strong (frame-level labels) training with balanced sampling, aggressive augmentation, and ensemble knowledge distillation — on top of SSL + AudioSet-Weak, for five transformers: ATST-Frame, BEATs, fPaSST, M2D, ASiT. Yields large PSDS1 gains on AudioSet-Strong eval and consistently better frame-level downstream fine-tuning. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.09546; code+checkpoints: https://github.com/fschmid56/PretrainedSED (MIT, verified). README verified: also ships low-complexity 'frame_mn10' (3.83M params) and 'frame_mn06' (1.62M) frame-level MobileNets, and the ensemble pseudo-labels for DIY distillation.
entries.params_and_speed: Transformers ~86-90M each; frame_mn06/10 are 1.62M/3.83M. inference.py runs SED on arbitrary-length audio; checkpoints auto-download.
entries.pretrained: All checkpoints in the GitHub release https://github.com/fschmid56/PretrainedSED/releases/tag/v0.0.1, MIT. Companion EfficientSED repo (https://github.com/theMoro/EfficientSED, arXiv:2503.11373) reports fmn10+transformer-head reaching PSDS1 45.25 vs 45.4-46.5 for SOTA transformers, at ~5M params, 40ms frames.
entries.relevance: This is the single most relevant resource for AutoSlicer v2: frame-level-pretrained checkpoints (the exact inductive bias you need), an MIT-licensed fine-tuning codebase with an arbitrary-length inference script, and a ready-made teacher(transformer)-to-student(frame_mn10) distillation path that fits 'train tonight on 3090s, deploy under 10GB'.

entries.name: ATST-Frame / ATST-SED (Audio-WestlakeU)
entries.key_idea: Self-supervised teacher-student transformer trained specifically for frame-level representations (frame-wise augmentation + masking, BYOL-style), AST-based; outperforms other SSL models by a large margin on frame-level SED, with 40ms temporal resolution vs BEATs' 160ms. Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.04186 (TASLP); SED fine-tuning recipe: https://github.com/Audio-WestlakeU/ATST-SED (MIT, verified), built on the DCASE 2023 Task 4 baseline.
entries.params_and_speed: ~86M (AST/ViT-B-based). Fine-tuning recipe is documented as batch-size sensitive (repo ablates this); works on 24GB-class GPUs with care.
entries.pretrained: SSL/weak checkpoints via Google Drive links in audiossl/ATST-SED repos; improved AudioSet-Strong version ATST-F_strong_1.pt in fschmid56/PretrainedSED (MIT).
entries.relevance: Top accuracy choice when frame timing matters: its 40ms resolution gives the cleanest song-boundary localization among the big transformers, and ATST-SED is a complete, MIT-licensed fine-tune-on-strong-labels recipe you can repurpose for 'A-singing / other-singing / none' classes.

entries.name: DCASE Task 4 recipe: CRNN + mean-teacher + frozen BEATs embeddings (baseline; FDY-LKA-CRNN winner)
entries.key_idea: The proven low-labeled-data SED recipe: a small CRNN consumes mel features concatenated with frozen BEATs frame embeddings (avg-pooled to match rates); mean-teacher EMA consistency on unlabeled/weak data; mixup. The 2023 winner (Kim et al., https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.06461) added frequency-dynamic convolution + large-kernel attention + BEATs embeddings + pseudo-labeling (PSDS1 0.591 / PSDS2 0.835, 1st place); 2024 follow-up added auxiliary decoder + max-probability aggregation (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.12721). Task overview: https://arxiv.org/html/2406.08056v1; results: https://dcase.community/challenge2023/task-sound-event-detection-with-weak-labels-and-synthetic-soundscapes-results.
entries.params_and_speed: CRNN head is ~1-5M trainable params on top of frozen ~90M BEATs; trains in hours on one 3090 because gradients only flow through the head. Inference dominated by BEATs forward pass — still far faster than real-time.
entries.pretrained: DCASE baseline code (BEATs+CRNN) at https://github.com/DCASE-REPO/DESED_task; BEATs checkpoint from microsoft/unilm; all MIT.
entries.relevance: This is the architecture pattern purpose-built for your data regime: ~10 classes, few hours of strong labels, lots of unlabeled audio (your VOD backlog is free unlabeled data for mean-teacher). Swap DESED classes for {A-sings, other-vocals, music-no-vocals, speech} and you have a near-turnkey design.

recommendations:
1) Architecture pattern: pretrained AudioSet backbone (frozen or LoRA/partially unfrozen) + small frame-level sequence head (CRNN or 2-layer transformer) + median-filter/HMM post-processing — this is the DCASE Task 4 consensus and replaces your 2021 SincConv+ResNeXt+BiGRU stack wholesale. Your 'needs minutes of context' problem disappears: AudioSet backbones see 10s windows and the sequence head + post-processing supplies song-level context cheaply.

2) Concrete picks (build 2, compare): (a) ACCURACY TRACK: fine-tune ATST-F_strong_1.pt or BEATs_strong_1.pt from fschmid56/PretrainedSED (MIT, auto-downloading checkpoints, arbitrary-length inference.py already written) with a 3-4 class frame head: {A-singing, other-singing/vocal-recording, music-without-vocals, speech/none}. Use the ATST-SED / DESED_task codebase with mean-teacher on your unlabeled VOD hours. ~90M params fine-tunes on one 24GB 3090 overnight (freeze lower blocks if tight). (b) SPEED TRACK: fine-tune frame_mn10 (3.83M, 40ms frames, PretrainedSED) or EfficientSED fmn10+TF — it reaches PSDS1 within ~1 point of the transformers at 5% of the params and will process a 6h VOD in well under a minute on a 10GB GPU. If the speed track gets within a few F1 points of the accuracy track on your validation streams, ship it; otherwise distill track-(a)'s logits on your VODs into fmn10 (the PretrainedSED repo ships the exact KD machinery).

3) The confusers (a)-(c) (pure/partial/mismatched instrumentals) are exactly the 'singing voice detection' decision that AudioSet-pretrained models already encode (Singing vs Music classes); with a few hours of channel-matched labels covering karaoke-backing-track-only segments, >90% frame accuracy is realistic. Confuser (d) — same song sung by ANOTHER singer played as a recording — is NOT solvable by any tagging backbone alone: it is singer identity verification. Add a second branch: pool a singer embedding (e.g., a speaker-verification model like ECAPA-TDNN or a singer-ID fine-tune of one of these backbones) over detected singing frames and score cosine similarity against an enrolled profile of streamer A built from confirmed clips; also exploit acoustic-channel cues (A sings through the live mic chain; played recordings are full-mix, mastered, often with their own backing). Label this confuser class explicitly in your training data.

4) Licensing: everything you need is permissive — EfficientAT/PretrainedSED/EfficientSED/HTS-AT/BEATs-code/EAT/ATST-SED are MIT (verified via GitHub API), PaSST Apache-2.0, AST BSD-3. One trap: CED's GitHub training code is GPL-3.0 (verified LICENSE file) while the HF mispeech/ced-* weights are tagged Apache-2.0 — if you use CED, load HF weights in your own training loop.

5) Training tonight on 3090s: start from the strong-label checkpoints (skip SSL and AudioSet-weak stages entirely); 64-128 10s crops per batch for fmn10, 16-32 for the transformers with bf16; mixup + SpecAugment + gain/codec augmentation (simulate stream audio chain); mean-teacher over unlabeled VOD segments. Expect head-only BEATs+CRNN to converge in ~2-4h, full fmn10 fine-tune in ~2-6h, ATST-F fine-tune overnight. Inference budget is a non-issue: even the 90M transformers run two-plus orders of magnitude faster than real time on 10s windows; mn10-class CNNs are ~4 orders (measured 4767 10s-clips/s on A100; scale down ~5-8x for a 10GB card).
